visits.text: New Glarus - Wisconsin's 'Little Switzerland.' Founded in 1845 by 108 immigrants from the Canton of Glarus, this town has taken its Swiss heritage all the way: flower-decked chalets, German signage, cheese factories, and bakeries. The historical museum recreates a nineteenth-century Swiss village.

New Glarus Brewing Company - Wisconsin's most cult brewery. Their Spotted Cow (a creamy farmhouse ale) is sold only in Wisconsin - making it a prized contraband item for Illinois neighbors. Free tour of the exterior and beer garden. Tastings at the bar.
Historical Museum - Several reconstructed buildings: chalet, cheese factory, forge, school. The story of Swiss immigration to Wisconsin is well told.

activities.text: Sugar River State Trail - This former rail line converted to bike trail crosses 24 miles of rolling countryside between New Glarus and Brodhead. It passes through a railroad tunnel and several bridges. Bike rentals available in the village.

Local Cheese Tasting - Wisconsin is America's top cheese producer. Around New Glarus, several artisan cheese factories offer tastings of gruyère, aged cheddar, and fresh cheese curds (the famous 'squeak-squeak' that squeaks under your teeth).

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: Yale University - One of America's Oldest and Most Prestigious Institutions: Founded with roots dating to the 1640s, this Ivy League campus spans 200 acres featuring buildings from three different centuries. Free student-led walking tours reveal Gothic treasures like Sterling Memorial Library and the iconic Harkness Tower rising 216 feet above the historic campus.

Yale University Art Gallery - The Nation's Oldest University Art Museum: Open since 1836, this world-class institution houses over 300,000 works spanning ancient civilizations to contemporary masters, all with free admission in a stunning Louis Kahn-designed building.

Yale Peabody Museum of Natural History - Newly Renovated Wonder: After four years of extensive renovations reopening in 2024, this museum showcases extraordinary collections including the famous Age of Reptiles mural and dinosaur specimens that have educated visitors for generations.

Marsh Botanical Garden - Eight Acres of Living Laboratory: This university-managed garden features greenhouses, outdoor collections, and research facilities supporting both scientific study and public enjoyment of horticultural diversity.

activities.text: New Haven Pizza Trail - Birthplace of American Pizza Perfection: This college town claims America's finest pizza tradition, with legendary establishments perfecting thin-crust pies since the early 1900s. The famous 'white clam pie' has achieved national recognition, while multiple pizzerias have earned spots on 'best in America' lists.

Louis' Lunch - Where the Hamburger Was Born: This tiny establishment claims to have served America's first hamburger sandwich, still prepared on original vertical cast-iron grills using a century-old recipe that draws pilgrims from around the world.

Long Wharf Food Truck Paradise - Latin American Street Food Hub: A cluster of vendors along Long Wharf Drive serves authentic tacos, empanadas, Cuban plantains, and other Latin American specialties from mobile kitchens.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven

visits.text: Avery Island Tabasco Factory — Tour the source of Tabasco pepper sauce produced on this salt dome island since 1868. The McIlhenny Company factory processes peppers grown on-site into the iconic sauce shipped worldwide in 195 countries.

Jungle Gardens — Wander through 67 hectares of botanical gardens featuring 900-year-old Buddha statue, bamboo groves, and lagoons. The sanctuary on Avery Island protects snowy egrets nearly hunted to extinction for their plumes in the 1890s.

Shadows-on-the-Teche — Explore this 1834 plantation home preserved by the National Trust for Historic Preservation on Bayou Teche's banks. The Classical Revival mansion retains original furnishings and documents four generations of the Weeks family.

Konriko Rice Mill — Discover how rice is processed at America's oldest operating rice mill and company museum. The 1912 facility explains Louisiana's rice industry history while demonstrating milling equipment still producing Konriko brand rice.

activities.text: Bayou Teche paddle — Kayak or canoe down Louisiana's most historic waterway winding through moss-draped cypress swamps. The scenic 201-kilometer bayou offers wildlife viewing and access to Cajun culture along its banks.

Boudin trail food tour — Sample different boudin recipes at specialist shops and gas stations along this unofficial culinary trail. Each establishment claims the best recipe for this essential Cajun sausage served hot from steamers.

New Iberia Main Street — Browse antique shops and cafes in this historic downtown preserving 19th-century commercial buildings. The compact Main Street reflects South Louisiana's French-Spanish heritage in its architecture and culture.

days_plan.itin_title: Louisiana Cultural Journey: New Orleans Jazz to Cajun Bayou Country
Deep Louisiana Road Trip: Jazz and Bayous

visits.text: Start at Jackson Square, the heart of the French Quarter since 1721. This 2.5-acre plaza, modeled after Paris's Place des Vosges, witnessed Louisiana's transfer from France to the United States in 1803 during the Louisiana Purchase. At the center stands an 1856 equestrian statue of General Andrew Jackson. Around you: artists display their work along the wrought-iron fence (200 permits issued annually), jazz musicians perform on the sidewalks, and tarot readers offer their services—all in a delightfully chaotic New Orleans fashion.

Facing the square, St. Louis Cathedral is the oldest continuously active Roman Catholic cathedral in the United States. The current building dates from 1789, rebuilt after the great fire that devastated the French Quarter in 1788. On either side stand the Cabildo (former seat of Spanish colonial government, now Louisiana State Museum) and the Presbytère (Mardi Gras museum). Cathedral admission is free—take a look at the rococo-style altar.

Cross Decatur Street to the French Market, the oldest public market in the United States (operating since 1791). You'll find stalls selling local produce, Cajun spices, hot sauce, and souvenirs. At the market's end, the old U.S. Mint building houses the New Orleans Jazz Museum—it's the only building in the U.S. to have served as a mint for both the United States and the Confederacy.

Walk up Royal Street—the French Quarter's gallery and antique dealer street, much quieter than Bourbon Street. The wrought-iron balconies adorned with ferns are a sight in themselves. Look for the initials forged into the railings: they identify the Creole families who built these houses in the early 19th century.

Take the St. Charles Avenue streetcar from Canal Street—the oldest continuously operating streetcar line in the world, designated a National Historic Landmark in 2014. The wooden cars date from 1923-1924, with no air conditioning—open windows are part of the charm. The ride costs $1.25 (exact change required). Get off at Washington Avenue to enter the Garden District.

The Garden District is the neighborhood of grand mansions built by wealthy Americans after the 1803 Louisiana Purchase. Unwilling to live with the Creoles in the French Quarter, they built their own palaces on the other side of Canal Street. You'll see Greek Revival, Italianate, and Queen Anne-style homes, shaded by centuries-old oaks draped with Spanish moss. Stroll Prytania, Coliseum, and First Streets for the finest facades.

Lafayette Cemetery No. 1, at the corner of Washington Avenue and Prytania Street, is one of New Orleans' famous "cities of the dead" cemeteries. Because the water table is so high, graves are built above ground in marble and brick vaults. This cemetery dates from 1833 and has been used as a filming location for several movies and series. Check opening hours before visiting—access is sometimes restricted.

Head down Magazine Street, parallel to St. Charles Avenue, for six miles of independent shops, galleries, and restaurants. This is the best spot for non-touristy shopping: antiques, vintage clothing, bookstores, contemporary art galleries. If you're hungry, po'boys (giant sandwiches stuffed with fried seafood or meat) are the specialty to order here.

Start the morning at Mardi Gras World, a massive warehouse on the Mississippi riverfront where Mardi Gras floats are built. The guided tour lasts about an hour and you'll see artisans at work on giant papier-mâché and fiberglass sculptures—some over 20 feet tall. New Orleans' Mardi Gras attracts more than a million visitors annually, and the floats you see here parade down St. Charles Avenue during carnival season (January-February).

Head to the National WWII Museum, ranked among America's finest museums. Founded in 2000 in New Orleans because this is where Andrew Higgins designed the landing craft used on D-Day. The museum spans several buildings and you could easily spend a full day—allow at least 2 to 3 hours for the main exhibitions. The planes, tanks, and submarines on display are authentic.

Late afternoon, ascend to Vue Orleans, the observation deck on the 34th floor of the former World Trade Center. The 360-degree view covers the Mississippi, French Quarter, Superdome, and Lake Pontchartrain. The interactive exhibit on the same floor traces the city's history through its various colonial periods.

Return route to New Orleans, but not via the interstate. Take US-90 eastbound then branch onto LA-1 along Bayou Lafourche, nicknamed "the longest cul-de-sac in the world"—a straight road following the bayou for dozens of miles, lined with houses on stilts and shrimp boats moored along the banks. Stop in Thibodaux, a small Cajun town on the bayou, for coffee and a photo of the historic downtown.

Arriving in the New Orleans area, make a detour through Barataria Preserve at Jean Lafitte National Historical Park (free admission, 30 minutes south of NOLA). Elevated wooden trails traverse a cypress and palmetto swamp where you can observe alligators, nutria, and water birds without needing a boat. The main trail is 2.2 miles and accessible to all.

Last day in New Orleans—spend it on what you've missed. If you haven't been to City Park yet, now's the time. This 1,300-acre park (larger than New York's Central Park) features 800-year-old oaks, the New Orleans Museum of Art (NOMA), and the Sydney & Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden—a contemporary sculpture garden outdoors among cypress trees and lagoons, free and open to all.

Take the Canal Street streetcar (City Park/Museum line) to get there—it's another historic line that crosses Mid-City, a residential neighborhood experiencing renewal with good restaurants and local bakeries.

activities.text: Late afternoon, settle into a café terrace overlooking Jackson Square for café au lait and beignets—the local specialty served here since the Civil War. Beignets are squares of fried dough covered in powdered sugar, always served in threes.

Evening, head to Frenchmen Street (not Bourbon Street—locals will tell you). This is where the city's real jazz clubs are concentrated. You can listen to live music in several clubs without paying cover—just walk from bar to bar and follow what moves you. Jazz was born here in New Orleans in the early 20th century, and Frenchmen Street is its beating heart today.

Continue by streetcar to Audubon Park (Calhoun Street stop). This 350-acre park lined with centuries-old oaks borders the Mississippi River. You can walk, jog, or simply sit beneath the trees. Audubon Zoo is at the back of the park if you're traveling with children.

Return by streetcar downtown and enjoy a free evening. If you want to explore the Creole culinary scene, restaurants in the Warehouse District (between the French Quarter and Garden District) offer a good balance of local cuisine and less touristy atmosphere than Bourbon Street.

Make a detour through the Tremé neighborhood, the oldest African American neighborhood in the United States. This is where enslaved people gathered on Sundays at Congo Square (now in Louis Armstrong Park) to play music and dance—a site considered one of jazz's birthplaces. The neighborhood gave its name to the HBO series.

Last evening in NOLA: if you haven't done it yet, take a stroll down Bourbon Street for the atmosphere (loud, touristy, but it's a must at least once). Bars are open around the clock and you can walk with your drink in the street—it's legal here, unlike most American cities.

Return to New Orleans late afternoon. Take advantage of this last evening to explore the Bywater neighborhood, east of the French Quarter. This is the city's creative district: giant murals, alternative galleries, cocktail bars in former warehouses. The atmosphere is less touristy than the French Quarter—this is where the city's artists and musicians live.

Final hours in NOLA: return to the French Quarter for one last café au lait, one last beignet, one last piece of jazz in the street. Louis Armstrong Airport (MSY) is 25 minutes by car from downtown. If you're returning your rental car, allow some margin—traffic on I-10 can be heavy during rush hour.

places.name: New York

visits.text: Start at the Frick Collection, 1 East 70th Street. This 1914 mansion once belonged to Henry Clay Frick, a steel magnate who spent 40 years assembling European masterpieces. You'll see three of the 36 known Vermeers in the world - including Officer and Laughing Girl, where window light illuminates the young woman's face while the officer remains in shadow. Don't miss Bellini's St. Francis in the Desert, considered by many art historians to be the finest painting in America, or Fragonard's The Progress of Love series - rejected by Madame du Barry, Louis XV's mistress, then purchased by Frick from J.P. Morgan. The rooms are never crowded: you see the paintings up close, without barriers. Reserve your timed tickets online.

Walk up Fifth Avenue to the Metropolitan Museum of Art at 82nd Street. With over 2 million works spanning 5,000 years, it's the largest art museum in the Americas. If you could only see one thing, head straight to the Temple of Dendur in the Sackler Wing - an Egyptian temple from 15 BC reassembled stone by stone in a glass-walled gallery overlooking Central Park. The Egyptian collections number 26,000 pieces, including the gilded sarcophagus of a priest of Amun. The Vermeers, Rembrandts and Caravaggios are on the second floor in the European galleries. In season, go up to the rooftop - the skyline view at sunset is one of the city's finest. Budget at least 2 hours, even rushing.

Continue to the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, 1071 Fifth Avenue. The building itself is the work of art - Frank Lloyd Wright spent 16 years designing it (1943-1959) and never saw the opening, dying six months before completion. The 400-meter spiral ramp takes you down through the collections in a continuous helix. You'll find Kandinskys (the world's largest collection of his works), Picassos, Mondrians, Chagalls and Modiglianis. The building has been a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2019. Take the elevator to the top and walk down: it's the best way to visit.

End the day at the Museum of the City of New York, 1220 Fifth Avenue at 103rd Street. Housed in a neo-Georgian building from 1932, it chronicles 400 years of the city's history. The scale model of future New York and Berenice Abbott's photo collection (Changing New York, 1935-1938) are worth the trip. The surrounding neighborhood - East Harlem - is less touristy and gives you a glimpse of a more authentic New York.

Begin at the American Museum of Natural History, Central Park West at 79th Street. Founded in 1869, it houses 34 million specimens. The star is the 94-foot blue whale suspended in the Milstein Hall of Ocean Life - a life-size model built in 1969. On the fourth floor, the dinosaur fossils include a nearly complete Tyrannosaurus rex skeleton and a 50-foot Barosaurus rearing up on its hind legs in the Theodore Roosevelt entrance hall. The Rose Center for Earth and Space, a 90-foot glass sphere, houses the Hayden Planetarium. Budget at least 2 hours, even moving quickly - the museum covers 2 million square feet.

Right across the street at 170 Central Park West, the New-York Historical Society is the city's oldest museum, founded in 1804. It holds one of the two remaining original copies of the Declaration of Independence printed on July 4, 1776, along with all 435 original watercolors from John James Audubon's Birds of America. The basement gallery, DiMenna Children's History Museum, is perfect if you're traveling with family. Less famous than its neighbors, the place is rarely crowded.

If you're traveling with children, make a stop at the Children's Museum of Manhattan, 212 West 83rd Street. Across five floors, interactive exhibits cover science, health and the arts - everything is hands-on, manipulable, testable. The EatSleepPlay space for under-4s is particularly well designed. Even without kids, you can tour it in 30 minutes to understand the American approach to learning through play.

Finish the day in Harlem with the National Jazz Museum in Harlem, 58 West 129th Street. It's a small museum, but the story is enormous: Harlem was the birthplace of jazz in the 1920s-1940s, with the Cotton Club, Savoy Ballroom and Apollo Theater just blocks away. The museum offers guided listening sessions, rare archival recordings of Duke Ellington, Billie Holiday and Charlie Parker, and live sessions some evenings. The surrounding neighborhood deserves a stroll - the brownstones on Lenox Avenue haven't changed in a century.

Start at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), 11 West 53rd Street. Founded in 1929, it's the world's first museum entirely dedicated to modern art. Van Gogh's The Starry Night (1889) is in gallery 5-5 on the fifth floor - go at opening time, the room fills up within an hour. Right nearby, you'll find Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907), Monet's Water Lilies (immersive room on the second floor) and Dalí's The Persistence of Memory with its melting clocks. The sculpture garden (Abby Aldrich Rockefeller Sculpture Garden) is a peaceful haven in the middle of Midtown. Budget at least 2 hours.

Walk to the Museum of Arts and Design (MAD), 2 Columbus Circle. This 5-story museum is devoted to contemporary craft - ceramics, jewelry, textiles, glass, wood. Exhibitions rotate frequently, but what never changes is the open studio on the 6th floor where you can watch artists work live. The top-floor restaurant offers panoramic views of Columbus Circle and the southwest entrance to Central Park.

Head down to Pier 86 on the Hudson River for the Intrepid Sea, Air & Space Museum. You board the USS Intrepid, a 900-foot World War II aircraft carrier that also served during the Cold War and Vietnam. On the flight deck, about thirty aircraft are on display, including an Air France Concorde (the only one you can tour in New York) and an A-12 Blackbird, the fastest spy plane ever built (Mach 3.35). Next to the carrier, the submarine USS Growler - one of the few Cold War missile submarines open to the public - can also be toured. Budget 1.5 hours.

If you're interested, stop by Madame Tussauds New York on 42nd Street, right in Times Square. It's pure entertainment: wax figures of American and international celebrities, from sports to cinema. The main draw is the building itself and the frenetic atmosphere of Times Square around it. You can do it in 45 minutes.

Start at the Whitney Museum of American Art, 99 Gansevoort Street, at the foot of the High Line in the Meatpacking District. Renzo Piano's 2015 building is a machine for looking at the city - the stepped terraces offer views of the Hudson and the New Jersey skyline. The museum holds over 26,000 works of 20th and 21st century American art: Edward Hopper (the world's largest collection of his work, with 3,600 pieces bequeathed by his widow), Georgia O'Keeffe, Jean-Michel Basquiat, Andy Warhol. The Whitney Biennial, organized since 1932, is the essential gathering for contemporary American art.

Take the subway to the Lower East Side for the Tenement Museum, 103 Orchard Street. Here, no display cases - you tour real apartments in an 1863 building where 7,000 immigrants lived between 1863 and 2011. The guided tours (mandatory, in English) recreate the living conditions of families from Ireland, Germany, Italy or China. One apartment has remained as it was in 1869 - original wallpaper, coal stove, toilets in the courtyard. Book in advance: slots fill up quickly.

Head to the far south of Manhattan, at Bowling Green, for the National Museum of the American Indian. It occupies the Alexander Hamilton U.S. Custom House (1907), a stunning Beaux-Arts building with ceilings painted by Reginald Marsh depicting the great explorers. Admission is free - it's a Smithsonian museum. The collections cover 12,000 years of indigenous peoples' history across the Americas, from Kwakwaka'wakw ceremonial masks of the Northwest Coast to Navajo textiles.

End with the National September 11 Memorial & Museum, at the foot of the former World Trade Center towers. At ground level, the two square pools occupy exactly the towers' footprints - water falls 30 feet into a central void. Underground, the museum preserves the original tower foundations, an 11-ton twisted steel column and the Survivors' Staircase, the stairway by which hundreds escaped. Plan 1.5 hours and prepare yourself emotionally - it's an intense visit.

Begin the day in Brooklyn with the Brooklyn Museum, 200 Eastern Parkway. New York's second-largest museum at 560,000 square feet, it's far less crowded than the Met - and that's a good thing. The Egyptian collections are among the best in the United States, with pieces dating back 5,000 years. Judy Chicago's The Dinner Party (1979), a monumental feminist installation with 39 place settings dedicated to famous women from history, has a permanent room on the 4th floor. The American art galleries and temporary exhibitions regularly receive critical acclaim.

Take the subway to Court Street station for the New York Transit Museum, housed in a decommissioned 1936 subway station in Downtown Brooklyn. On the original platforms, about twenty vintage subway cars are lined up - from 1907 to today. You can board, sit on the wicker benches of early 20th century cars and compare them with modern trains. The museum also traces the construction of the New York subway, begun in 1900: 236 miles of track, 472 stations, 1.6 billion rides per year. Kids love it - and so do adults.

Finish the marathon with the Ellis Island National Museum of Immigration. Take the ferry from Battery Park - the combined ticket includes the crossing, access to Ellis Island and the Statue of Liberty pedestal. Between 1892 and 1954, 12 million immigrants passed through this building - over 40% of current Americans can trace an ancestor through Ellis Island. The Great Hall on the second floor, with its vaulted Guastavino tile ceiling, is where each arrival was inspected - those who didn't pass the medical exam were sent back. Search for your family name in the free database - you might be surprised. The ferry passes by the Statue of Liberty on the return: enjoy the view from the deck.

activities.text: Between museums, take advantage of Central Park. The entrance at 72nd Street (Bethesda Terrace) is a stone's throw from the Frick. Late in the day, stroll down Madison Avenue for its galleries and grab a drink at one of the Upper East Side bars - Gilded Age atmosphere guaranteed.

After the jazz museum, if you're there on a Wednesday or Saturday evening, check the Apollo Theater schedule (253 West 125th Street) - the Wednesday Amateur Nights have been running since 1934. Ella Fitzgerald was discovered there at 17.

Take advantage of your Times Square visit to watch the city's spectacle at nightfall - the giant screens and crowds are part of the New York experience. Grab a slice of pizza standing at the counter, like a real New Yorker.

After the Tenement Museum, explore Orchard Street and the Lower East Side on foot. It's the historic immigrant neighborhood: Jewish delis, vintage shops, street art. Try a pastrami sandwich at one of the century-old delis in the area - it's a New York institution.

Before taking the ferry, stroll through Battery Park and walk along the harbor esplanade. The view of the Statue of Liberty, Governors Island and New Jersey is superb in the morning. On the way back, if you still have energy, walk across the Brooklyn Bridge - 1.1 miles over the East River, with Manhattan as backdrop.
