visits.text: Hearst Castle — Explore the extravagant palace with 56 bedrooms and 61 bathrooms built between 1919 and 1947 for press magnate William Randolph Hearst. Architect Julia Morgan created this Hispanic-Moorish fantasy perched on La Cuesta Encantada, the enchanted hill.

Elephant Seal Vista Point — Observe the northern elephant seal colony sprawling lazily on the beaches of Piedras Blancas. These marine mammals that can weigh up to 2 tons have gathered here for molting and breeding since 1990.

Morro Rock — Contemplate this 176-meter volcanic dome emerging majestically from the ocean. Formed 23 million years ago, this volcanic neck sacred to the Chumash shelters nesting peregrine falcons and marks the entrance to the bay.

Mission San Luis Obispo de Tolosa — Visit this mission founded in 1772 by Father Junípero Serra, fifth in the chain of California missions. Its museum traces the tumultuous history of interactions between Franciscans and the native Chumash population.

activities.text: Stroll through the Farmers Market — Wander through the Thursday evening farmers market that has transformed Higuera Street into a lively street festival since 1983. Local producers, street musicians and barbecue vendors create a festive atmosphere typically Californian.

Wine tasting in Edna Valley — Wind through the rolling vineyards planted with Pinot Noir and Chardonnay in this renowned wine appellation. Family tasting rooms offer food and wine pairings in a bucolic setting 8 kilometers from downtown.

Hiking to Bishop Peak — Climb to the summit of this 474-meter volcanic peak, highest point of the Nine Sisters. The steep 6.5-kilometer trail rewards the effort with panoramic views of the valley and sparkling ocean.

days_plan.itin_title: Driving the Pacific Coast Highway: A Golden Gate Bridge to Hollywood Road Trip
The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Old Mission Santa Barbara — Discover the queen of California missions founded in 1786, the only one to have remained under continuous Franciscan administration. The neoclassical church with its double-tower facade houses a golden altarpiece and Chumash wall frescoes.

Santa Barbara County Courthouse — Admire this Spanish-Moorish architectural masterpiece completed in 1929 after the destructive 1925 earthquake. The El Mirador clock tower offers a 360° view of the red tile roofs and sparkling Pacific.

State Street — Stroll the main palm-lined shopping artery where designer boutiques, art galleries and fusion restaurants sit alongside Spanish colonial style buildings. The uniform architecture creates a Mediterranean atmosphere unique in California.
Stearns Wharf — Walk along California's oldest wooden pier built in 1872. Artisan shops and the Sea Center aquarium occupy the historic buildings while fishermen and pelicans share the docks.

activities.text: Wine tasting on the Urban Wine Trail — Explore the thirty or so tasting rooms concentrated in the Funk Zone, a former industrial district converted into an urban wine paradise. Winemakers from Santa Ynez Valley present their award-winning vintages in renovated warehouses.

Beach bike ride — Pedal along the bike path running 5 kilometers of golden sand beaches lined with palm trees. Stop at East Beach for an impromptu beach volleyball match or a surfing session in the gentle waves.

Sunset at Butterfly Beach — Settle in on this exclusive Montecito beach where celebrities blend incognito with locals. The sun plunging into the Pacific behind the Channel Islands creates memorable flaming skies.

days_plan.itin_title: Driving the Pacific Coast Highway: A Golden Gate Bridge to Hollywood Road Trip
The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Santa Cruz - California's Original Surf City: This laid-back university town at the northern tip of Monterey Bay has shaped surf culture since the sport arrived on the mainland in 1885. The world-famous break at Steamer Lane draws elite surfers while beginners learn on gentler waves at Cowell's Beach.

Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk - California's Last Remaining Seaside Amusement Park: This 1907 landmark operates two National Historic Landmarks: the 1924 Giant Dipper wooden roller coaster and the 1911 Looff Carousel. Free admission lets visitors soak up the vintage atmosphere without riding.

Natural Bridges State Beach - Where Monarchs Meet the Sea: This coastal park features a dramatic natural stone arch emerging from the surf, and from October through February, eucalyptus groves shelter thousands of migrating monarch butterflies.

activities.text: Mystery Spot - Where Gravity Gets Confused: This roadside attraction has baffled visitors since 1939 with optical illusions and seemingly impossible physical phenomena in a small patch of redwood forest. Kitsch or science experiment? You decide.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Marvel at Saint Francis Cathedral Basilica, a magnificent Romanesque Revival structure whose beautiful interior and stunning stained glass windows create an atmosphere of profound peace and artistic grandeur. The cathedral stands as the spiritual heart of Santa Fe and represents one of the finest examples of ecclesiastical architecture in the American Southwest.

Visit the Loretto Chapel, renowned for its mysterious spiral staircase that appears to defy architectural principles, constructed without any central support and using only wooden pegs. Legend attributes its creation to a mysterious carpenter who appeared to answer the sisters' prayers and vanished without payment upon completion. The tiny chapel and its miraculous staircase draw visitors from around the world.

activities.text: Stroll through Santa Fe's central Plaza, the historic heart of America's oldest capital city, surrounded by stunning adobe architecture, vibrant street performers, and exceptional artisan galleries. The plaza has served as the city's commercial and cultural center since its founding in 1610, making it one of the most historically significant public spaces in the United States.

Explore the Railyard Park district, where preserved railway heritage meets contemporary arts and culture in a revitalized urban space. Old railway details and industrial architecture provide atmospheric backdrops for galleries, markets, and community gatherings that celebrate Santa Fe's creative spirit.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

places.name: Santa Monica

visits.text: Wigwam Motel (Rialto/San Bernardino) - 19 concrete teepee-shaped rooms, built in 1949, surrounded by vintage cars. One of Route 66's most photographed motels. If you're not staying overnight, a photo from the parking lot is enough.

Santa Monica Pier - The 'End of the Trail' sign (installed in 2009) marks the official end of Route 66. You've driven 2,448 miles from Chicago. The Pacific is before you. Get your photo near the sign, then turn around: the Pacific Park Ferris wheel, the historic 1922 carousel, and arcade are there. It's the end of the journey and the beginning of California.

Venice Beach - Walk 2 miles south of the pier to Venice Beach, California's most eccentric boardwalk: bodybuilders working out outdoors, street performers, skaters, vendors selling everything and anything. The original Muscle Beach has been here since 1934.

activities.text: Pacific sunset - You've crossed the Illinois plains, Texas, the New Mexico and Arizona deserts, the Mojave. Sit on the Santa Monica sand and watch the sun descend into the Pacific. It's the perfect ending.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Los Angeles
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica

map_keywords: Santa Monica Pier End of Route 66

visits.text: Sarasota - Where Beach Vibes Meet Big City Culture: This Gulf Coast gem combines laid-back coastal living with world-class cultural institutions, creating an unusually sophisticated beach destination.

The Ringling - A Compound of Museums Celebrating Art and Circus: John Ringling's legacy includes his massive art collection, the Museum of the Circus, and Ca' d'Zan-his lavish Venetian Gothic mansion overlooking Sarasota Bay.

Siesta Key - America's Best Beach Sand: The quartz crystal sand of Siesta Key Beach stays remarkably cool even in blazing heat, a unique quality that has earned it 'best beach' titles from numerous publications.
Lido Beach - Siesta's Quieter Neighbor: Less crowded but equally beautiful, Lido Key offers a more laid-back alternative with the same stunning Gulf water.

St. Armands Circle - Upscale Shopping in a Garden Setting: This circular shopping district features upscale boutiques, galleries, and restaurants arranged around a beautifully landscaped central park.

activities.text: Siesta Key Village - Beachside Bohemia: Browse surf shops, sample local brews at hole-in-the-wall bars, and soak up the relaxed atmosphere of this beloved beach community.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Fall under the spell of Savannah, one of America's most enchanting cities, where cobblestone streets wind past 22 oak-shaded squares draped in Spanish moss beneath grand antebellum mansions. Founded in 1733, the city's original grid plan created the intimate green spaces that define its romantic character today. The blend of architectural styles spanning three centuries creates streetscapes of extraordinary beauty and historical significance.

Explore the hauntingly beautiful Bonaventure Cemetery, where ornate Victorian monuments and moss-draped oaks create an atmosphere of melancholic grandeur overlooking the Wilmington River. The oak-canopied avenue through Wormsloe Historic Site, lined with 400 live oaks planted in the 1890s, ranks among the most photographed roads in the American South. The elegant Mercer-Williams House offers tours through Savannah's storied past.

activities.text: Stroll along River Street where historic cotton warehouses now house eclectic shops, restaurants, and lively entertainment venues overlooking the Savannah River. Sunset paddlewheel cruises offer views of the brick waterfront accompanied by live jazz and Southern cuisine. Ghost tours after dark reveal the city's supernatural reputation as one of America's most haunted destinations.

Wander through the lush landscapes of Forsyth Park, anchored by an iconic 1858 fountain modeled after fountains in Paris and Cusco. The 30-acre park provides a green oasis for picnics, concerts, and people-watching beneath centuries-old trees. The surrounding Victorian district features some of Savannah's finest residential architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: Pike Place Market — Explore America's oldest continuously operating public market founded in 1907, where fishmongers toss salmon and vendors sell fresh flowers, artisan cheeses, and local crafts. The nine-acre complex contains over 225 businesses across multiple levels descending toward Elliott Bay.

Space Needle — Ascend 184 meters to the observation deck of Seattle's iconic 1962 World's Fair landmark designed by John Graham & Company. The rotating glass floor and 360-degree views encompass Puget Sound, Mount Rainier, the Cascades, and the Olympic Mountains.

Chihuly Garden and Glass — Marvel at Dale Chihuly's spectacular blown glass sculptures displayed in indoor galleries and outdoor garden beneath the Space Needle. The Glasshouse centerpiece features a 30-meter suspended sculpture of red, orange, and yellow forms.

Seattle Art Museum — Discover collections spanning ancient to contemporary art in this downtown museum founded in 1933. The building by Robert Venturi houses Northwest Coast Native art, European paintings, and contemporary installations across 312,000 square feet.

Museum of Flight — Explore the largest independent air and space museum on the West Coast with over 175 aircraft and spacecraft. Highlights include the original Air Force One, a Concorde, and the Space Shuttle Trainer housed in six connected buildings.

Ballard Locks — Watch boats navigate between Puget Sound and Lake Union through this engineering marvel completed in 1917. The fish ladder allows visitors to observe salmon migrating upstream while ship traffic passes through massive lock chambers.

Fremont neighborhood — Discover Seattle's self-proclaimed Center of the Universe featuring quirky public art including the Fremont Troll sculpture under the Aurora Bridge. Vintage shops, brewpubs, and Sunday markets create bohemian atmosphere.

activities.text: Original Starbucks visit — Sip coffee at the first Starbucks opened in 1971 at Pike Place Market, maintaining its original brown mermaid logo. The flagship store preserves the company's coffee roasting heritage before global expansion.

Underground Tour — Descend beneath Pioneer Square streets to explore the buried 1890s city preserved after the Great Seattle Fire of 1889. Guided tours reveal original storefronts and sidewalks now one story underground after street levels were raised.

Waterfront evening stroll — Walk along Elliott Bay past the Seattle Great Wheel and ferry terminals as the sun sets behind the Olympic Mountains. The revitalized waterfront features restaurants, shops, and public art with Puget Sound views.

Coffee culture tour — Visit specialty roasters and cafes showcasing Seattle's coffee obsession beyond Starbucks. Third-wave coffee shops emphasize single-origin beans, pour-over methods, and latte art.

Sunset cruise on Puget Sound — Glide past islands, ferries, and container ships as the Olympics turn pink in alpenglow. Evening cruises offer views of the Seattle skyline illuminated against mountain silhouettes.

days_plan.itin_title: Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes
Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes

places.name: Sedona

visits.text: Ascent from Havasupai (10 miles, pre-dawn start), then 4.5-hour drive to Sedona. You'll arrive tired—that's normal. Go straight to Airport Mesa for sunset: this 360° viewpoint overlooks Sedona's red sandstone formations with the green mountains of Coconino National Forest in the background. The parking lot is small—arrive 30 minutes before sunset.
If you still have energy, visit Tlaquepaque Arts & Shopping Village—an artists' village built in Mexican colonial style, with galleries, pottery studios, and restaurants under sycamore trees.

Cathedral Rock is THE Sedona hike. The trail is 1.2 miles one-way (short but steep—750 feet of elevation gain), with sections where you scramble on all fours up sandstone. From the saddles at the top, the view of Sedona's red formations and Oak Creek valley is spectacular. Start early—the parking lot fills before 8am.

In the afternoon, do the Bell Rock & Courthouse Butte loop (3.7 miles, easy). Bell Rock is a perfectly bell-shaped formation—you can climb its lower slopes. Courthouse Butte is more massive and the trail circles it. It's one of Sedona's vortexes.

Take AZ-89A north to drive through Oak Creek Canyon—nicknamed the "Grand Canyon's little cousin." The road winds for 12 miles between red cliffs covered in pines. Stop at Slide Rock State Park—a natural 80-foot waterslide in sandstone polished smooth by Oak Creek water. Kids (and adults) love sliding in the icy water.

Heading back to Sedona, stop by the Chapel of the Holy Cross—a church embedded in a red sandstone cliff, built in 1956. The architecture is striking: a 90-foot cross integrated into the glass and concrete façade, with red rock as the back wall. The view from the parking lot is already beautiful, but go inside—the light-flooded interior is worth the visit.

activities.text: Sedona is known for its "vortexes"—points where earth energy is supposedly concentrated. The four main vortexes are at Airport Mesa, Cathedral Rock, Bell Rock, and Boynton Canyon. Whether you believe in them or not, they're also Sedona's best hiking spots.

If you want another hike, Devil's Bridge Trail (4 miles round-trip) leads to Sedona's largest natural sandstone bridge—50 feet wide suspended above the void. The photo on the bridge went viral on social media. The trail isn't difficult but the Dry Creek Road parking lot fills fast.

The Red Rock Scenic Byway (AZ-179) between Sedona and Village of Oak Creek is one of Arizona's most beautiful scenic roads—7.5 miles with Bell Rock, Courthouse Butte, and Cathedral Rock as backdrop. Drive it in late afternoon when the sun grazes the red formations.

Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

map_keywords: Chapel of the Holy Cross Sedona

visits.text: Make a sweet pit stop at Alabama's Peach Capital, where the whimsical Peach Water Tower rises above Interstate 65 like a giant fruit beckoning travelers to pull over. At Headley's Big Peach farm shop, bins overflow with fragrant just-picked peaches while freezers stock peachy popsicles in multiple mouthwatering flavors. Nearby, Peach Park and Durbin Farms Market compete for visitors with peach cobblers, preserves, and ice cream that capture summer in every spoonful.

Begin your Selma pilgrimage at the Interpretive Center nestled at the foot of the Edmund Pettus Bridge, where rangers and volunteers share the full story of the 1965 Voting Rights marches. Audio-visual displays and artifacts bring those turbulent days to vivid life, from the planning sessions at local churches to the violent confrontation on Bloody Sunday. This marks the official start of the Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail.

Walk across the Edmund Pettus Bridge, feeling history beneath your feet on the same span where marchers for voting rights were beaten back by state troopers on March 7, 1965. The images of Bloody Sunday, broadcast worldwide, shocked America's conscience and galvanized support for the Voting Rights Act. Two subsequent marches followed, culminating in 25,000 people completing the 54-mile journey to Montgomery's Capitol steps.

Enter the twin-towered Brown Chapel AME Church, the spiritual and strategic headquarters where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and fellow organizers planned their nonviolent campaign for the ballot. Marchers assembled here before crossing the bridge, and wounded protestors returned here for aid after being tear-gassed and clubbed. The monument to Dr. King at the church entrance honors his pivotal role in the struggle.

Step back into antebellum elegance at Sturdivant Hall, a magnificent Greek Revival mansion built in 1852 that showcases the wealth generated by Alabama's cotton economy. Columned porticos and graceful proportions frame rooms filled with period antiques and fine art, while the old kitchen houses a gift shop brimming with Southern treasures. The manicured gardens offer a peaceful contrast to the weighty history elsewhere in Selma.

activities.text: Drive slowly through Selma's Old Town Historic District, where antebellum mansions line quiet streets shaded by ancient oaks draped in Spanish moss. Though some homes show their age, the architectural grandeur hints at the prosperity this river port once enjoyed as a center for cotton shipping and Confederate manufacturing. Each columned porch and wrought-iron balcony tells a story of the complicated South.

days_plan.itin_title: Alabama Deep South Itinerary: Civil Rights, Music and Gulf Coast

visits.text: Jour 1: Alaska SeaLife Center - This marine research facility and public aquarium in Seward provides close encounters with Alaska's marine wildlife, including Steller sea lions, harbor seals, sea otters, and seabirds like puffins and murres. The center conducts important research on marine ecosystems and wildlife rehabilitation.

Jour 1: Exit Glacier - The only road-accessible portion of Kenai Fjords National Park features trails leading directly to the face of this actively retreating glacier. The 0.8-mile trail to the glacier face passes markers showing the ice's position in previous years, dramatically illustrating glacial recession. The strenuous Harding Icefield Trail climbs 3,500 feet over 8.2 miles to views of the vast ice field feeding Exit Glacier and dozens of others.

Jour 1: Seward Historic Waterfront - This picturesque port town at the head of Resurrection Bay was established in 1903 as the ocean terminus of the Alaska Railroad. The small boat harbor, galleries, and historic buildings reflect the community's fishing heritage and role as gateway to Kenai Fjords.

Jour 2: Kenai Fjords National Park - This 670,000-acre park protects the Harding Icefield, one of the four largest ice caps in the United States, and the spectacular coastal fjords carved by its glaciers. Tidewater glaciers calving into the sea create a landscape of extraordinary beauty and ecological richness.

Jour 2: Holgate Glacier and Aialik Bay - Full-day cruises venture deep into the park's fjords to witness massive tidewater glaciers actively calving icebergs into the sea. The thunderous crack and splash of ice breaking from glacier faces creates one of nature's most dramatic spectacles.

Jour 2: Northwestern Fjord - Extended cruises reach this remote arm of the park, where multiple glaciers flow from the Harding Icefield and wildlife viewing opportunities multiply with distance from Seward.

activities.text: Jour 1: Resurrection Bay Kayaking - The protected waters of the bay offer excellent paddling with mountain and glacier views, wildlife observation opportunities, and access to remote beaches and coves.

Jour 2: Kenai Fjords Wildlife Cruise - Half and full-day boat tours explore the park's coastal ecosystems, with regular sightings of humpback whales, orcas, sea otters, Steller sea lions, and harbor seals. Seabird colonies host thousands of puffins, cormorants, and kittiwakes on rocky islands and cliffs.

Jour 2: Fishing Charters - The nutrient-rich waters of Resurrection Bay and adjacent Gulf of Alaska support excellent fishing for halibut, salmon (kings, silvers, and sockeye), lingcod, and rockfish throughout the summer season.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

visits.text: Behold Tsé Bitʼaʼí (Shiprock), a dramatic volcanic rock formation rising over 1,500 feet above the surrounding desert plain on Navajo Nation land. This sacred site holds profound spiritual significance to the Navajo people, who consider it a place of great power and cultural importance. The jagged peak and radiating dikes create one of the most distinctive natural landmarks in the American Southwest.

Learn about the geological and cultural significance of this ancient volcanic neck, the eroded remnant of a volcano that erupted approximately 27 million years ago. The formation's English name derives from its resemblance to a 19th-century clipper ship, while Navajo traditions associate it with a great bird that carried their ancestors to safety. Photography from surrounding viewpoints captures the monument's dramatic presence against endless desert skies.

activities.text: Experience the vast landscapes of the Navajo Nation, the largest Native American reservation in the United States, where traditional culture and natural beauty interweave across millions of acres of high desert terrain. Respect for sacred sites and Navajo customs enriches any visit to this remarkable region.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

visits.text: Head to Sioux Falls, South Dakota's largest city. Falls Park is the heart of town: the Big Sioux River cascades over pink quartzite formations 1.7 billion years old. In season, 14,000 liters of water per second rush over the rocks. Climb the observation tower (5 stories) for an overview, then descend onto the walkways at water level. The ruins of an old 1881 stone mill add a photogenic touch to the scene.

Downtown Sioux Falls has completely reinvented itself in recent years. SculptureWalk is an outdoor trail of over 60 contemporary sculptures installed along the sidewalks—they change annually. Phillips Avenue is the main street, with pink quartzite buildings (the same stone as the falls) converted into restaurants, galleries, and bars.

activities.text: In the evening, sample Sioux Falls' food scene—the city has exploded gastronomically with chefs highlighting Great Plains products: bison, sweet corn, wild berries. For local beer, downtown craft breweries abound.

places.name: South Bend

visits.text: University of Notre Dame - The 520-hectare campus merits a stroll even if you're not a college football fan. The Golden Dome (1879), covered in gold leaf, dominates the main avenue. The Basilica of the Sacred Heart, in neo-Gothic style, possesses 44 stained-glass windows imported from France in the 19th century. In front of the stadium, the wall of the Hesburgh Library displays "Touchdown Jesus," a 40-meter-high mosaic visible from the stands.

Studebaker National Museum - Studebaker manufactured cars in South Bend from 1902 to 1963, but the company had existed since 1852 as a wagon maker. The museum displays models ranging from Lincoln's presidential carriage to the last Avanti. Allow 1 hour.
The History Museum - Right next to the Studebaker, this museum traces the history of the St. Joseph River valley. The Kidsfirst section is well designed for families.

activities.text: River Lights Plaza in the evening - On the banks of the St. Joseph River downtown, this light installation changes color and intensity according to the water's movements. In the evening, the riverside promenade is pleasant and uncrowded.

days_plan.itin_title: Indiana Circuit: Dunes, Caves, and Cities

map_keywords: University of Notre Dame South Bend

visits.text: Discover downtown Spartanburg's revitalized urban core, where recent development has created a walkable district suited to relaxed exploration. The compact downtown divides into distinct neighborhoods, each offering its own character and dining options. Historic architecture mixes with contemporary additions in this evolving Upstate community.

activities.text: Experience the Hub City's emerging cultural scene, where local establishments reflect regional tastes in comfortable settings. The downtown area has transformed into a destination for travelers seeking authentic small-city Southern atmosphere. Spartanburg's position along major routes makes it an ideal stopping point between coastal and mountain destinations.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Long drive west across the Iowa prairies—3.5 hours of corn and soybean fields stretching to the horizon. You arrive at the Iowa Great Lakes, the state's resort area. West Okoboji Lake is renowned for its clarity—it's one of only three blue water lakes in the world formed by glaciers, along with Lake Geneva and a lake in Manitoba. Settle onto the beach at Arnolds Park in early afternoon.

Arnolds Park Amusement Park, open since 1889, is Iowa's oldest operating amusement park. This isn't Disneyland—it's a retro lakeside carnival with wooden roller coasters, vintage rides, and a "1950s American summer" vibe. Next door, the Iowa Great Lakes Maritime Museum traces the region's nautical history.

activities.text: Late in the day, drive around the lake or rent a pontoon to cruise the water. The sunset over West Okoboji is one of Iowa's most beautiful—locals compare it (with considerable enthusiasm) to those of Lake Como.

places.name: Spring Green

visits.text: Taliesin - Frank Lloyd Wright's residence, studio, and 800-acre estate in the Driftless Area hills. The name comes from Welsh meaning 'shining brow' - Wright built his house on the hillside, not the summit, because 'if you build on top of the hill, you lose the hill.' Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2019, it's Wisconsin's only publicly accessible UNESCO site. Budget 2-4 hours depending on the tour chosen.
Hillside Home School (1903) - The school Wright designed for his aunts, with its enormous assembly hall and diamond-paned windows. Later became the Taliesin apprentice architects' workshop.
Romeo and Juliet Windmill (1896) - One of Wright's early commissions. The structure of interlocking diamond and octagon was so bold his uncle bet it wouldn't stand. It's still there, 130 years later.
Riverview Terrace Café - Wright's last restaurant design in the world. Panoramic windows overlooking the Wisconsin River. Local cuisine, relaxed atmosphere. Make it a lunch stop.

activities.text: Taliesin Guided Tour - Four tour options, from 1h to 4h. The 'Highlights' tour (2h) offers the best time-to-content ratio: you visit the school, Wright's residence, and studio. The 'Estate' tour (4h) covers the entire grounds including Midway Barn and gardens. Booking essential.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: Zion Canyon Scenic Drive - The 6-mile road climbs into the canyon via free shuttle bus (mandatory April through October). Navajo Sandstone cliffs soar 2,000 feet on either side. First stop: Court of the Patriarchs, three monoliths named Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob by a Methodist minister in 1916. The view from the platform is one of the park's most photographed scenes.

Emerald Pools - Three tiered pools fed by springs seeping through the sandstone. The Lower Pool is an easy 20-minute walk. If you have the energy, continue to the Upper Pool - the waterfall drops 100 feet from a ledge into a red rock amphitheater. Allow 1.5 hours for the full loop.

Continue by shuttle to the Temple of Sinawava at the end of the road. This natural temple takes its name from the Paiute coyote-god. Here the canyon narrows to just 20 feet wide and 2,000 feet deep - the gateway to The Narrows. The Riverside Walk, a paved 1-mile trail along the Virgin River, leads you to the canyon entrance. Even without wading in, the stroll is magnificent.

Angels Landing OR The Narrows - Today is built around a choice. Both hikes are long and demanding; doing both in one day is only realistic for highly experienced hikers. Choose the one that suits you. Option 1: Angels Landing. The trail climbs 1,500 feet over 2.5 miles one-way. Walter's Wiggles - 21 steep switchbacks carved into the rock in 1926 - haul you up to Scout Lookout. From there, the final section follows a narrow ridge with chains as the only safety measure and sheer drops on both sides - a 1,500-foot fall. A permit is mandatory (online lottery system). If you don't have a permit or suffer from vertigo, Scout Lookout already offers spectacular canyon views. Start early in the morning.

Weeping Rock - After the effort of Angels Landing, this short 0.5-mile trail leads to a rock overhang where water seeps constantly. The water you see dripping is actually rainfall from 1,200 years ago, filtered slowly through the sandstone. It's cool, refreshing, and takes 20 minutes.

activities.text: In late afternoon, wander through the village of Springdale. Art galleries and outdoor patios line the road leading to the park entrance. If you're planning to do The Narrows tomorrow, stop by an outfitter to rent neoprene socks, a walking stick, and waterproof pants - essential gear if the water is cold or the current strong.

Option 2: The Narrows from the bottom - If you prefer canyons to heights, or if you don't have an Angels Landing permit, enter The Narrows from the Temple of Sinawava. You'll walk directly in the Virgin River between 2,000-foot walls just a few yards apart. No permit needed for the bottom-up out-and-back route - go as far as you like and turn around. Allow 3 to 5 hours depending on your ambition. Warning: flash floods are deadly. Don't attempt this hike if storms are forecast. If you're a strong hiker and leave at dawn, you can attempt both - but plan for a 10-hour day and bring plenty of water.

If you have time in late afternoon, take the Zion-Mount Carmel Highway eastward. The 1.1-mile tunnel carved through the sandstone between 1927 and 1930 is an engineering feat - windows cut into the rock offer dizzying views into the canyon. On the other side, don't miss Checkerboard Mesa, a sandstone dome striped with horizontal and vertical lines that resembles a giant chessboard.

visits.text: Begin your Appalachian Trail experience at Springer Mountain, the legendary southern terminus of the 2,190-mile footpath stretching to Maine. Georgia's 79-mile section winds through misty forests, wildflower meadows, rhododendron tunnels, and offers spectacular panoramic overlooks. Standing at the bronze plaque marking Mile Zero creates a profound connection to one of America's greatest hiking traditions.

Trek to Blood Mountain, the highest point on Georgia's Appalachian Trail at 4,458 feet, where ancient stone shelter ruins and sweeping ridge views reward determined hikers. The challenging terrain passes through diverse ecosystems supporting black bears, deer, salamanders, and countless songbirds. Backcountry camping sites offer stargazing far from light pollution.

activities.text: Visit the legendary Mountain Crossings outfitter at Neel Gap, the only building the Appalachian Trail passes directly through. The famous 'shoe tree' outside displays worn-out hiking boots left by through-hikers, many containing handwritten notes and memories. Detour to misty Long Creek Falls for a serene waterfall tucked into the forest.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

places.name: Springfield

visits.text: Gemini Giant (Wilmington, IL) - This 28-foot fiberglass giant holding a Gemini rocket is the first of the 'Muffler Men' you'll encounter on the Route. Built in the 1960s to attract motorists, it stands in front of a former restaurant. Quick photo stop.

Pontiac Murals - Don't pass through without stopping - this small Illinois town has transformed its walls into an open-air gallery. Over 20 murals tell the story of Route 66. The Route 66 Hall of Fame Museum is right next door - free admission.

Abraham Lincoln Home (Springfield) - The only house Lincoln ever owned, preserved by the National Park Service. Pick up a free ticket at the visitor center for the guided tour - first come, first served. The entire neighborhood is preserved as it was in the 1860s.

Gateway Arch (St. Louis) - This 630-foot stainless steel arch, the tallest structure in the United States when it opened in 1965, marks the Gateway to the West. You can ride to the top in tiny capsule-like trams (book online). Below, the museum traces westward expansion. Even if you don't go up, the view from the park along the Mississippi is impressive.

Chain of Rocks Bridge - Stop on this abandoned 1929 bridge over the Mississippi - it has a quirk: it makes a 22-degree bend right in the middle. Now reserved for pedestrians and cyclists, it's one of the most photogenic spots on the Route. Free entry.

Meramec Caverns (Stanton, MO) - These caves, 60 miles southwest of St. Louis, were supposedly used as a hideout by outlaw Jesse James according to local legend. The guided tour lasts 1h20 and descends 60 feet underground. Temperature stays constant at 60°F - bring a jacket.

activities.text: Lunch at the Cozy Dog Drive In - This is where the corn dog (deep-fried cornmeal-battered sausage on a stick) was invented in 1946. The restaurant is still run by the same family and the walls are covered with Route 66 memorabilia.
Frozen treat at Ted Drewes - This St. Louis institution since 1929 serves frozen custard so thick the server flips the cup upside down to prove it won't fall out. The line is part of the experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Los Angeles
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica

Ted Drewes Frozen Custard St Louis

visits.text: Castillo de San Marcos — Explore America's oldest masonry fort constructed 1672-1695 from coquina limestone by Spanish colonial forces. The star-shaped fortress withstood multiple sieges with its unique shell-rock walls that absorbed cannonballs rather than shattering.

St. Augustine Lighthouse — Climb 219 steps up this 1874 lighthouse for panoramic views of America's oldest continuously occupied European settlement. The 50-meter tower's first-order Fresnel lens still guides ships along Florida's northeast coast.

Colonial Quarter — Walk through four centuries of St. Augustine history in this living history museum. Costumed interpreters demonstrate blacksmithing, leather working, and colonial crafts in reconstructed 16th-18th century buildings.

St. George Street — Stroll this pedestrian-only colonial thoroughfare lined with Spanish architecture housing galleries, restaurants, and shops. The brick-paved street retains its historic character with balconies, courtyards, and gas lanterns.

activities.text: Fountain of Youth Archaeological Park — Visit the legendary spring where Ponce de León supposedly searched for eternal youth in 1513. The 15-acre park preserves the Timucua Native American village site and Spanish colonial artifacts.

Ghost tour of historic district — Join an evening walking tour through shadowy streets hearing tales of pirates, soldiers, and spirits haunting America's oldest city. Guides recount 450 years of history in the candlelit colonial quarter.

Sunset at the Old City Gates — Watch the sun set through the reconstructed 1739 entrance gates to the colonial city. The coquina pillars frame views down St. George Street as the historic district glows in golden light.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida's Diverse Coast: Everglades Adventure to Miami Art Deco
The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Charlevoix - A driving day with three stops, covering about 125 miles total between Traverse City and St. Ignace. First stop: Charlevoix, a harbor town wedged between Lake Michigan and Lake Charlevoix, 45 minutes from Traverse City. It's worth the detour for its "Mushroom Houses" - 28 stone cottages with undulating roofs and whimsical mushroom shapes, built between 1919 and 1973 by Earl Young, a self-taught builder who never obtained an architecture degree. He worked without blueprints, using limestone blocks and boulders hauled from Lake Michigan. Pick up a walking tour map at the visitor center on Mason Street - the finest examples cluster along Park Avenue and Clinton Street.

Petoskey - Gaslight District - Continue on US-31 to Petoskey, a resort town perched on Little Traverse Bay. The historic Gaslight District, listed on the National Register of Historic Places, contains 278 period buildings and about 170 shops and restaurants. Look for the Ernest Hemingway statue - he spent summers here in his youth and frequented the City Park Grill, which still operates today. Don't leave without collecting a "Petoskey stone" from the beach - this 350-million-year-old coral fossil is Michigan's official state stone.

Mackinac Bridge - After Petoskey, reach Mackinaw City to cross the Mackinac Bridge, nicknamed the "Mighty Mac." Opened in 1957, this suspension bridge measures 5 miles total (26,372 feet). Its suspension span between anchorages (8,614 feet) makes it the longest suspension bridge in the Western Hemisphere by this measure - though its main span between towers (3,800 feet) ranks third in North America, behind the Golden Gate and Verrazzano-Narrows. Engineer David Steinman designed it to withstand violent strait winds: the deck can shift laterally 35 feet under extreme gusts, and the roadway surface is open-grated to let wind pass through. Five workers died during the 42 months of construction. The crossing takes about 10 minutes by car - enjoy views of Lake Michigan to the left and Lake Huron to the right.

Mackinac Island - Take the ferry from St. Ignace (15-minute crossing) to reach this island where cars have been banned since the 1920s. Everything happens on foot, by bicycle, or in horse-drawn carriages. The island welcomes nearly a million visitors per year but counts only 600 permanent residents. Upon docking, you'll arrive in a Victorian-fronted village lined with fudge shops - it's been the local specialty for over a century.

Grand Hotel - Perched on a bluff overlooking the straits, this 1887 hotel boasts the world's longest porch: 660 feet long, lined with rocking chairs. Five U.S. presidents have stayed here. Thomas Edison gave the first public demonstration of his phonograph here. The film "Somewhere in Time" starring Christopher Reeve was shot here in 1980. At minimum, climb up to the porch for the view - a small admission fee is charged if you're not a hotel guest.

Fort Mackinac - This fort built by the British in 1780 towers over the harbor from a limestone bluff. It played a role in the War of 1812 between Americans and British. You'll find 14 restored period buildings, cannon-firing demonstrations in season, and panoramic views of the straits and Mackinac Bridge in the distance.

Arch Rock - Continuing around the island on foot or by bike, don't miss this natural limestone arch perched 150 feet above Lake Huron. Formed by erosion, it measures roughly 50 feet across. A 207-step staircase leads up from the coastal road.

activities.text: Petoskey Stone Hunting at Petoskey State Park - Before leaving the area, stop at Petoskey State Park. The beach pebbles hide Petoskey stones - hexagonal Devonian coral fossils. Wet the stones to reveal the pattern - it shows up better on a damp surface. Allow 30 to 45 minutes for a beach stroll.

Bike Loop Around the Island - The coastal road runs 8.2 miles and traces Lake Huron around the island's entire perimeter. It's flat, easy, and the scenery alternates between limestone cliffs, forest, and beaches. Bike rentals are available near the dock. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours without stops.

Sunset from Straits State Park - Back in St. Ignace, head to this small state park to watch the sun set behind the towers of Mackinac Bridge. The silhouette of the illuminated bridge at twilight is one of Michigan's most photogenic images.

days_plan.itin_title: Michigan Great Lakes Road Trip: Dunes and Shorelines
Michigan Great Lakes Road Trip: Dunes and Shorelines

places.name: St Louis

visits.text: First stop on Route 66: Pontiac, 100 miles south of Chicago. The Route 66 Hall of Fame & Museum occupies a former fire station and showcases memorabilia, neon signs, and vintage vehicles. Outside, the town's walls are covered with murals - about twenty to spot as you wander the streets. Allow 45 minutes for the museum and murals.

In Springfield, stop at Oak Ridge Cemetery to pay respects at Abraham Lincoln's tomb. The 117-foot granite monument houses the remains of the 16th president and three of his four sons. At the entrance, rub the nose of Lincoln's bronze statue - it's a local tradition said to bring good luck, and the worn bronze proves it.

Still in Springfield, pass by the Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library & Museum. Even without entering, the building and plaza are worth seeing. If you have time, the interior uses special effects and recreations to tell Lincoln's story - it's one of the nation's highest-rated presidential museums.

activities.text: Between Pontiac and Springfield, take time to drive old Route 66 sections rather than the interstate. You'll encounter abandoned gas stations, vintage diners, and murals painted on grain silos. The Mother Road atmosphere remains intact on these stretches - keep your camera handy.

days_plan.itin_title: Chicago to New Orleans Road Trip: Route 66 & the Mississippi

map_keywords: Route 66 Hall of Fame Museum Pontiac Illinois

visits.text: St. Petersburg - The Sunshine City: This bayside community consistently breaks records for consecutive sunny days, combining beach-town relaxation with an increasingly sophisticated arts scene.

The Dalí Museum - The World's Most Comprehensive Salvador Dalí Collection: This striking waterfront museum houses over 2,400 works by the surrealist master, including seven of his 18 monumental 'masterwork' paintings.
St. Pete Murals - An Open-Air Gallery: Over 700 murals transform the city into one of America's largest outdoor art exhibitions, with new works constantly appearing on buildings throughout downtown.
The Don CeSar - The Pink Palace of St. Pete Beach: This iconic 1928 resort, painted its distinctive Pepto-Bismol pink, has hosted everyone from F. Scott Fitzgerald to Franklin D. Roosevelt.
Fort De Soto Park - Five Interconnected Islands of Paradise: This 1,136-acre park on Tampa Bay's southern tip offers camping, fishing, hiking, and some of the most beautiful beaches in Florida.

activities.text: St. Pete Beach Water Sports - Gulf Coast Adventures: The calm, warm waters of the Gulf of Mexico provide ideal conditions for paddleboarding, parasailing, and kayaking.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Explore St. Simons Island, the largest of Georgia's Golden Isles, where wide golden beaches, moss-draped oaks, shimmering salt marshes, and charming village atmosphere create quintessential coastal Georgia scenery. The historic lighthouse dating to 1872 offers panoramic views from its tower after climbing 129 steps. Over 30 miles of bike paths wind past tree spirits carved into ancient oaks by local artists.

Visit Fort Frederica National Monument, the substantial ruins of a colonial-era British fortification established in 1736 to defend against Spanish incursion from Florida. The moss-covered walls and archaeological excavations reveal 18th-century military life on the frontier. Cannon's Point Preserve offers hiking through wild maritime forest where salty marsh aromas drift on ocean breezes.

activities.text: Relax on sunlit East Beach, browse boutiques and galleries in the charming Pier Village, and watch spectacular sunsets paint the marshlands in brilliant colors. The island's laid-back atmosphere invites visitors to slow down and embrace the gentle rhythms of Georgia's coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

days_plan.itin_title: Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey

visits.text: Stillwater—Founded in 1843, this is Minnesota's oldest town. Walk down Main Street toward the St. Croix River: 19th-century brick buildings now house bookstores, antique shops, and restaurants. Don't miss the Stillwater Lift Bridge (1931), a working vertical lift bridge that spans the river into Wisconsin.

Walk down to the banks of the St. Croix—this clear-running river, a tributary of the Mississippi, is designated a Wild and Scenic River. The forested bluffs on both sides give the valley the feel of a miniature fjord.

activities.text: Canoe or kayak the St. Croix River—outfitters offer rentals right on the water. The current is gentle, the banks wild. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a relaxed float.
Stillwater is known for its bookstores. Step inside one—some occupy vaulted brick cellars. If you're a wine enthusiast, several wineries have set up in the valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Minnesota in 10 Days: Twin Cities, Lakes, and Lake Superior's North Shore

visits.text: Sturgeon Bay - Door County's 'gateway' is also a shipbuilding town. The canal cutting the peninsula in two connects Green Bay to Lake Michigan. Downtown is pleasant with art galleries, restaurants, and shops.

Door County Maritime Museum - The history of shipbuilding and Great Lakes navigation. Models, tools, and shipwreck stories (Door County owes its name to the dangerous currents of 'Death's Door,' the passage between the peninsula and Washington Island).

Cana Island Lighthouse - 30 minutes north of Sturgeon Bay. Cross the gravel causeway (sometimes flooded) to this 9-acre island. The 89-foot lighthouse dates from 1869. Climb the 97 steps for the view. Door County has 11 lighthouses total - more than any other Wisconsin county.

activities.text: Wine and Cider Tasting - Door County has several wineries and cideries. The peninsula's cherries produce fruity wines and easy-drinking ciders. Ask to try cherry wine, the local specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: Prepare for the hike to Havasupai—one of the most spectacular and difficult-to-access places in the United States. From Hualapai Hilltop, the trail first descends 2,600 feet in 1.5 miles of switchbacks, then follows a sandy wash for 7 miles to the village of Supai—the country's most isolated town, 700 residents, accessible only on foot, horseback, or helicopter. Mail still arrives by pack mule.

Two miles past the village, Navajo Falls appears on your left—a series of turquoise waterfalls amid red sandstone. Then Havasu Falls, the star: a 100-foot turquoise waterfall tumbling into a pool surrounded by travertine. The color comes from calcium carbonate dissolved in the water. This is the image you've seen all over Instagram—and in person, it's even better.
The campground is just past Havasu Falls, along Havasu Creek. Set up your tent anywhere in the designated area—first come, best spot. The creek water is 70°F year-round.

Hike down to Mooney Falls, half a mile from the campground. It's Havasupai's tallest waterfall—200 feet of freefall. To access it, you descend through a passage in the rock with chains and metal ladders bolted to the wall. It's steep, slippery, and vertiginous—but the turquoise pool at the bottom is worth the effort.

Third day at Havasupai—savor it. Explore Fifty Foot Falls, a more discreet waterfall between the village and Havasu Falls, often overlooked by hurried hikers. The pool is smaller and more intimate. The rest of the day, swim in Havasu Creek, read by the water, photograph the changing light on the cliffs. This is why you came.

activities.text: A permit is mandatory—3-night minimum, about $455 per person. Reservations open February 1st and sell out in minutes. No day hiking allowed. Leave before dawn to avoid heat—carry at least 3 liters of water. No services on the trail. This is true backcountry.

If you're a strong hiker, continue 3.5 miles beyond Mooney Falls to Beaver Falls—a series of terraced waterfalls with natural swimming pools. The trail follows Havasu Creek with several stream crossings. Allow 4 hours round-trip from the campground. The rest of the time, swim in the pools—the 70°F water is perfect after hiking.

Mentally prepare for tomorrow: the climb back to Hualapai Hilltop is 10 miles with 2,600 feet of elevation gain. Leave before dawn—heat in the wash is brutal after 9am. Some hikers book a helicopter for the ascent ($80 per person), but seats are limited and flights don't start until 10am.

Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

places.name: Superior

visits.text: Big Manitou Falls - Wisconsin's highest waterfall awaits you 20 minutes south of Superior. The Black River plunges 165 feet (50 meters) into a basalt gorge carved 1.2 billion years ago by lava flows. Native Americans called it 'Gitchee Manitou' - the voice of the Great Spirit. Multiple viewing platforms offer unobstructed views from both sides of the gorge. Arrive early morning to beat the crowds and catch the best light.

Little Manitou Falls - Two miles upstream, the river splits into two channels and drops 31 feet (9 meters) across a wide basalt shelf. Less dramatic than its big sister, but more accessible: you can scramble down to the pool to feel the spray. Watch for slippery rocks.

Interfalls Lake and Beach - Between the two falls, the Black River slows and forms this man-made lake bordered by pines and spruces. The sandy beach (trucked in from Lake Superior shores in the 1930s by the Civilian Conservation Corps) makes for a pleasant swimming break.

CCC Buildings - The Civilian Conservation Corps transformed this park between 1935 and 1941. The cut-stone shelter, nature center, and former bathhouse still stand. Interpretive signs tell the story of these young workers who came primarily from Illinois.

activities.text: Big Manitou - Little Manitou Loop Trail - This 4-mile trail follows the Black River and passes both waterfalls. Moderate elevation gain (215 feet). In fall, maples and birches frame the gorge in red and gold.

visits.text: Talkeetna Historic District - This charming frontier village at the confluence of three rivers has served as the traditional base camp for Denali climbers since the early 1900s. The compact Main Street preserves authentic gold rush-era architecture, with original wooden storefronts now housing local artisans and outfitters. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places, the town offers an authentic glimpse into Alaska's pioneering heritage.

activities.text: Denali Flightseeing - Experience breathtaking aerial views of North America's highest peak and the surrounding Alaska Range. Small aircraft circle massive glaciers, navigate between towering granite spires, and offer perspectives of Denali's 20,310-foot summit that few ever witness. Weather permitting, glacier landings provide the opportunity to step onto ancient ice fields.

Susitna River Rafting - Float through pristine wilderness where the Susitna, Talkeetna, and Chulitna rivers converge. These glacially-fed waterways wind through boreal forest with views of the Alaska Range, offering opportunities to spot moose, eagles, and salmon depending on the season.

Talkeetna Zipline Tours - Soar above the Talkeetna River valley on aerial cables stretching through old-growth spruce forest, with panoramic mountain views serving as the backdrop to this elevated wilderness experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

visits.text: Marvel at Tallulah Gorge, a dramatic 2-mile-long chasm plunging nearly 1,000 feet deep where five magnificent waterfalls cascade over ancient rock formations. A suspension bridge spans the gorge 80 feet above the rushing Tallulah River, providing heart-stopping views of the canyon walls and roaring cascades. Free permits allow adventurous hikers to descend to the gorge floor for an intimate encounter with this geological wonder.

Explore over 20 miles of hiking and biking trails radiating from the gorge rim, each offering different perspectives of the dramatic landscape carved over millennia by rushing waters. The park's historic dam and interpretive center document the area's role in early hydroelectric development. Overlooks along the rim trail provide photographers with stunning compositions of waterfalls and canyon walls.

activities.text: Experience the exhilarating natural waterslide at Sliding Rock, where smooth granite channels visitors into refreshing plunge pools surrounded by moss-covered boulders and echoing canyon walls. The nearby lake beach offers calmer swimming for those seeking relaxation after gorge adventures.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: Tampa Bay - Where Theme Parks Meet Championship Sports: This Gulf Coast metropolis combines world-class attractions with a thriving food and drink scene that rivals any city in Florida.

Busch Gardens Tampa Bay - Theme Park Meets World-Class Zoo: This unique combination of African wildlife preserve and thrill ride destination features one of the country's best roller coaster collections alongside remarkable animal habitats.

Historic Ybor City - Tampa's Latin Quarter Since 1885: Once the cigar capital of the world, this National Historic Landmark District preserves Cuban, Spanish, and Italian heritage through architecture, restaurants, and the ongoing cigar-rolling tradition.
Tampa Riverwalk - 2.6 Miles of Waterfront Wonder: This continuous pedestrian path connects downtown museums, parks, restaurants, and attractions along the Hillsborough River and Tampa Bay.

activities.text: Tampa Craft Beer Trail - Florida's Brewing Capital: Pioneering breweries like Cigar City and Coppertail have established Tampa Bay as a craft beer destination, with dozens of taprooms offering local brews.
Tampa Bay Sports - Championship Caliber: Home to the NFL's Buccaneers and NHL's Lightning-both recent champions-the region offers professional sports events year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Experience Taos Pueblo, a UNESCO World Heritage Site with over 1,000 years of continuous habitation, representing one of the oldest continuously inhabited communities in North America. The remarkable multi-story adobe structures, some reaching five stories, have been home to the Tiwa-speaking Puebloan people since before European contact. Visitors can explore the living village and learn about traditions that have survived for a millennium.

Discover the quaint mountain town of Taos, nestled beneath stunning peaks and surrounded by artistic heritage that has attracted creative spirits for over a century. The surrounding Wheeler Peak Wilderness Area offers challenging hiking and skiing opportunities with breathtaking views from New Mexico's highest summit at 13,167 feet.

activities.text: Experience the thrill of rafting down the Rio Grande through rugged, wild terrain where the river has carved dramatic gorges through the volcanic landscape. Outfitters offer trips ranging from gentle floats to challenging whitewater adventures through some of the most spectacular canyon scenery in the Southwest. The Rio Grande del Norte National Monument protects this remarkable landscape for future generations.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

visits.text: Temecula Valley Wine Country - Southern California's Napa: Nearly 50 wineries grow over 30 varietals in this warm-climate region, producing distinctive wines that have earned national recognition. Rolling hills dotted with oaks create a surprisingly Tuscan atmosphere.

Old Town Temecula - The Real Old West: This six-block historic district preserves 1880s wooden storefronts where cowboys once hitched their horses. Today, antique shops, restaurants, and wine tasting rooms fill the authentic buildings.

Temecula Valley Balloon & Wine Festival - Rise Above the Vines: This annual June event sends dozens of colorful hot air balloons floating over wine country, combining morning flights with wine tastings and live entertainment.

activities.text: Hot Air Balloon Sunrise Flights - Float Over Wine Country at Dawn: Year-round morning launches drift over patchwork vineyards as mist rises from the valleys, followed by traditional champagne toasts upon landing.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

places.name: Terlingua

visits.text: Ross Maxwell Scenic Drive - This 30-mile road cuts through the heart of Big Bend descending toward the Rio Grande. Stop at Sotol Vista for a 180-degree panorama over the desert and mountains of Mexico. Further down, Tuff Canyon offers a short trail above a canyon carved through volcanic rock.

Santa Elena Canyon - At the end of the road, the Rio Grande plunges into a 1,500-foot-high canyon. A 1.6-mile trail crosses Terlingua Creek (sometimes you'll wade if water's high) and hugs the base of the cliff. The Mexican wall is within earshot on the other side of the river - you're literally at the border.

Terlingua Ghost Town - Leaving the park, stop in this abandoned mining town. The 1900s adobe ruins now serve as a backdrop for a few bars, restaurants, and galleries. The atmosphere at sunset, with raking light on the ruins and desert, is unique.

Chisos Basin - Wind up the serpentine road (7 miles, 1,700 feet elevation gain) to this mountain basin nestled in the heart of Big Bend. At 5,400 feet elevation, the temperature is 20°F cooler than the desert below. The visitor center is here, along with the park's only lodge.

Window Trail - From Chisos Basin, this 5.6-mile round-trip trail descends into a canyon to an opening (the 'window') that frames the desert below. The view through this gap in the rock is Big Bend's signature photo. Set out late afternoon for sunset through the window.

Rio Grande Village - Take the nature trail (0.75 miles) that climbs a small hill. From the top, you'll see the river below and the Mexican village of Boquillas del Carmen across - the border has never seemed so close.

Boquillas Canyon Trail - This 1.4-mile trail descends to the Rio Grande then follows the base of a 1,000-foot-high canyon. The hike is easy but the heat can be fierce - set out early. From the riverbank, you might spot Boquillas residents offering handmade crafts from the other shore.

activities.text: Grab a drink on a Terlingua bar terrace at sunset - the locals, a mix of desert drifters and adventurous retirees, are welcoming and the stories are worth the stop.

Lost Mine Trail - If you'd rather climb than descend, this 4.8-mile round-trip trail ascends along a ridge with panoramic views over the Chisos Basin and the Rio Grande desert. It's the park's highest-rated hike - start early to beat the heat.

Big Bend is a certified Dark Sky Park - at night, the total absence of light pollution makes the Milky Way appear like a river overhead. Step outside after dinner and let your eyes adjust to the darkness for 20 minutes.

Hot Springs Trail - This 1-mile trail leads to a natural hot spring (105°F) right on the bank of the Rio Grande. You sit in a stone pool with Mexico on the other side of the river. It's one of the few places in the world where you can soak in a hot spring while looking at another country. Go early morning or late afternoon - in full sun it's too hot.

If the border crossing is open (check at the visitor center), you can cross the Rio Grande by rowboat to visit the Mexican village of Boquillas del Carmen - passport required. It's a tiny village of 150 people. Otherwise, enjoy your last evening in the Terlingua desert.

places.name: Three Rivers

visits.text: The General Sherman Tree is the largest living tree on the planet - by trunk volume. It stands 83.8 meters tall, 11 meters in diameter at the base, and contains 1,487 cubic meters of wood. It's between 2,200 and 2,700 years old, meaning it was already a young tree when Rome was founded. It's neither the oldest (that title goes to a bristlecone pine in the Sierra Nevada) nor the tallest, but no other tree disputes its mass. Notable fact: it continues growing each year, adding the equivalent of an 18-meter tree's worth of new wood. The paved trail from the parking lot descends 500 meters to the tree - the return is uphill, so take your time as the 2,100-meter elevation makes itself felt.

Moro Rock is a granite dome that rises from the sequoia forest like a ship's prow. The stone staircase carved into the rock climbs in 300 steps to the summit at 2,050 meters elevation - allow 20 minutes. The 360° view of the Great Western Divide and Kings Canyon gorge is worth every step. Don't miss the Tunnel Log on the road to Crescent Meadow either: a sequoia that fell naturally in December 1937, through which park staff carved a tunnel in 1938 to let cars pass - it measures 2.4 meters wide and 8 meters high.

activities.text: Stop by the Giant Forest Museum before leaving the forest. The building dates from 1928 and houses exhibits on giant sequoia ecology - why they resist fires (their bark can reach 60 centimeters thick and doesn't burn), how they reproduce only through fire, and what we know about their shallow roots that intertwine with neighboring trees to share stability. In 2021, a fierce wildfire threatened the General Sherman - rangers wrapped its base in reflective fire-retardant sheeting normally reserved for structures. The tree survived undamaged.

Take the Congress Trail starting from the General Sherman: a 5-kilometer loop that passes through dozens of giant sequoias, several with diameters larger than your living room. The forest is silent, the ground soft beneath the needles, and the light filtered through the canopy is photographically rare. Allow 2 hours for the complete loop.

visits.text: Topeka - Where American History Changed Forever: Kansas's capital city preserves one of the most significant sites in the Civil Rights movement alongside stunning state architecture.

Brown v. Board of Education National Historic Site - The School That Changed America: The former Monroe Elementary School, where the landmark 1954 Supreme Court desegregation case originated, now serves as a powerful museum with a restored kindergarten classroom, compelling photographs, videos, and ranger-led programs explaining this watershed moment.

Brown v. Board Legacy Mural - 130 Feet of Civil Rights History: Directly across from Monroe Elementary, this massive mural depicts the ongoing legacy of the Supreme Court decision that ended legal segregation in American schools.

Kansas State Capitol - Taller Than Washington's Dome: This magnificent structure soars 17 feet higher than the U.S. Capitol in Washington, D.C., featuring an inner dome with 256 glass panels and a 900-pound chandelier that dazzles visitors who tilt their heads upward.

activities.text: Capitol Dome Tour - Climb to Breathtaking Views: Guided tours ascend to the dome for panoramic views of Topeka and insight into Kansas's governmental history.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: Fruita Historic District - As you enter Capitol Reef, you suddenly pass through a ribbon of green in the middle of the red desert. It's not a mirage: it's Fruita, a settlement founded in the 1880s by Mormon pioneers who planted orchards along the Fremont River. Never more than 10 families lived here - the last resident left in 1969. The National Park Service now maintains over 2,700 original fruit trees: cherries, apricots, peaches, apples. From June through October, you can pick the fruit yourself (honor-system scale and payment box at orchard entrances).

Gifford Homestead - This restored farmhouse amid the orchards sells homemade fruit pies, ice cream, and artisanal jams. Don't leave without trying the cherry or peach pie - it's one of Utah's national parks' best-kept secrets. Open mid-March through October.

Fremont Petroglyphs - Along Highway 24, a wooden boardwalk leads to a panel of petroglyphs carved by the Fremont people 1,000 to 2,000 years ago. You'll see bighorn sheep, human figures, and geometric shapes. Access is easy and free - it's 1.2 miles east of the visitor center.

Capitol Reef Scenic Drive - This 8-mile road runs along the western face of the Waterpocket Fold, a 100-mile geological wrinkle that cuts through the entire park from north to south. The name "Capitol Reef" comes from the white Navajo Sandstone domes that reminded early explorers of the U.S. Capitol, and the impassable cliffs they compared to reefs blocking ships. The road passes beneath 600-foot walls of red Wingate Sandstone - pull over often, each turn reveals a new vista.

Grand Wash Trail - At the end of a driveable dirt road, this flat 2.25-mile trail threads through a canyon with vertical walls that narrow in places to just a few yards. It's one of only four passages through the Waterpocket Fold. Easy and spectacular.

Hickman Bridge - This 133-foot natural sandstone bridge is accessible via a 1-mile trail from Highway 24. The climb is short but steep. Along the way, you'll pass the ruins of an ancient Fremont granary, tucked into an alcove.

activities.text: Cohab Canyon Trail - This 1.75-mile out-and-back trail climbs steeply above Fruita and offers a bird's-eye view of the orchards and the Waterpocket Fold cliffs. The name comes from "cohabitation": according to local lore, polygamous Mormons hid here to evade federal marshals in the 1880s. The ascent is steep at first, but the canyon at the top is beautiful.

Capitol Gorge Trail - Before the modern road was built in 1962, this narrow canyon was the only vehicle route through the Waterpocket Fold. Wagon and car travelers passed through here, and many carved their names in the rock - you'll see the "Pioneer Register" with inscriptions dating to the 1870s. The trail is 2 miles out-and-back, flat and easy. If you continue a bit farther, you'll reach the "water pockets" - natural potholes carved in the sandstone that hold rainwater for months.

In late afternoon, stop at the base of Chimney Rock, an isolated sandstone pillar visible from Highway 24. Capitol Reef is a certified Dark Sky Park - if you stay after sunset, the minimal light pollution makes it one of Utah's best stargazing spots.

visits.text: Front Street and Historic Downtown Traverse City - Start with a stroll down Front Street, the main thoroughfare lined with Victorian buildings converted into shops and tasting rooms. Along the way, spot the City Opera House at No. 106: built in 1891, it was the city's first building to have electricity and still hosts performances today. Traverse City is dubbed the "Cherry Capital of the World" - the surrounding five counties produce 40 to 50 percent of the nation's tart cherry harvest. If you're here in July, the National Cherry Festival has been running since 1925 with eight days of festivities, including a parade and pie-eating contests.

Old Mission Peninsula - Head north along this 18-mile finger of land that separates the two arms of Grand Traverse Bay. The lighthouse at the peninsula's tip sits almost exactly on the 45th parallel - halfway between the equator and the North Pole. All along the drive, you'll pass cherry orchards and vineyards - the sandy soil and Lake Michigan's microclimate are perfect for riesling and pinot gris. Stop at one of the wineries for a tasting.

Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore - Thirty minutes west of Traverse City, this national park protects 35 miles of Lake Michigan shoreline, established in 1970. The name comes from an Ojibwe legend: a mother bear fleeing a fire in Wisconsin swam across the lake with her two cubs. The cubs drowned from exhaustion and became North and South Manitou Islands. The mother, asleep on the bluff waiting for them, was covered in sand by the Great Spirit. The "perched" dunes rise 450 feet above the lake - formed 12,000 years ago as glaciers retreated.

Pierce Stocking Scenic Drive - This 7.4-mile loop winds through forest before emerging at spectacular overlooks. At Overlook #9, you'll discover the dunes' dizzying plunge toward Lake Michigan's blue-green waters, with the Manitou Islands on the horizon. Pierce Stocking, a land speculator who fell in love with the area, created this drive in the 1960s before the park was officially designated.

Dune Climb - This is the park's most popular attraction: a vast 130-foot sand ramp you can scramble up on foot. If you have the energy, continue beyond the crest - a 3.5-mile trail leads all the way to Lake Michigan through forest and dunes. Stay on marked paths: vegetation is fragile and protected.

Glen Haven Historic Village - This restored lakeside hamlet once housed a maritime rescue station in the 19th century. You'll find the Coast Guard building, a blacksmith shop, and D.H. Day's original general store, all telling the story of Great Lakes maritime and timber commerce. Admission is free.

activities.text: Sunset at Clinch Park Beach - Settle in facing Grand Traverse Bay to watch the sun sink over Lake Michigan. The water turns shades of turquoise striking enough to rival tropical seas. Pack a picnic with local cherry products - jam, sausage, or pie.

Walk along the Boardman River - Follow the pedestrian trail that traces the river through downtown. In late afternoon, light filters through the trees and the atmosphere turns peaceful. The main section takes about 30 minutes.

Empire Bluff Trail Hike - This short loop (1.5 miles round-trip) climbs through hardwood forest to a stunning panorama over South Bar Lake, Lake Michigan, and the dunes. Best in late afternoon for golden light. Moderate difficulty, allow 45 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Michigan Great Lakes Road Trip: Dunes and Shorelines
Michigan Great Lakes Road Trip: Dunes and Shorelines

visits.text: Perrot State Park - Limestone bluffs rise 500 feet above the Mississippi. The name 'Trempealeau' comes from French 'la montagne qui trempe à l'eau' - the mountain that soaks in the water - an isolated peak rising from the floodplain like an island. From the bluff tops, the view across the Mississippi Valley is vast: meanders, wooded islands, and transport barges.
Trempealeau Mountain - Climb to the summit of this 400-foot remnant butte for 360° views of the Trempealeau and Mississippi rivers' confluence. The trail is 2 miles round-trip.
Trempealeau River Confluence - At the mountain's base, the river joins the Mississippi in a calm meander. Marshy area good for birding.

activities.text: Kayaking Trempealeau Bay - Paddle through backwaters and channels of the Mississippi. The water is calm here, lined with willows and populated by herons and bald eagles. Outfitters rent kayaks at the water's edge.
Bluff Hiking - Brady's Bluff Trail (1.5 miles round-trip, 500 feet elevation gain) is the most popular trail. The climb is steep but short, and the summit reward is worth it.

places.name: Tucson

visits.text: Saguaro National Park East (Rincon Mountain District) sprawls across more than 67,000 acres east of Tucson. The saguaros surrounding you can reach 50 feet tall, weigh up to 2 tons when full of water, and live 200 years—the oldest here witnessed the Civil War. Start with the Cactus Forest Loop Drive, a paved 8-mile loop running through the heart of the cactus forest with multiple photo stops.

Mission San Xavier del Bac, about twenty minutes south of Tucson, is the oldest continuously active Catholic mission in the United States. Its construction began in 1783—its white domes contrast with the surrounding brown desert in a striking way. The baroque interior, covered with frescoes and gilded statues, is open to the public daily.

activities.text: Set out on the Mica View Trail (about 2.5 miles round trip, easy) from the picnic area of the same name: you walk among the saguaros without encountering many people, the cactus silhouettes cutting against the Rincon Mountains behind you. Ideal in late afternoon when the light softens.

Settle in at a park viewpoint for sunset—the silhouettes of saguaros against a backdrop of pink and orange sky are what most people come here for. Mica View Picnic Area is one of the best spots for this, away from the crowds.

places.name: Tucumcari

visits.text: Cadillac Ranch (Amarillo) - Cross the field (400 meters from the parking lot) to see ten Cadillacs planted nose-first in the ground, all covered in multicolored graffiti. This 1974 installation, financed by eccentric millionaire Stanley Marsh, pays tribute to the evolution of the Cadillac tail fin. Bring spray paint - this is one of the few places in the world where graffiti is encouraged. You'll need to walk 400 meters across the field from the parking area.
MidPoint Cafe (Adrian, TX) - You're exactly halfway between Chicago and Los Angeles: 1,139 miles on each side. The sign in front of the cafe confirms it. Try the Ugly Crust Pie - the house specialty.

Blue Swallow Motel (Tucumcari) - Arrive at dusk: the blue neon swallow promising '100% Refrigerated Air' is the most beautiful lighted sign on all of Route 66. This 1939 motor court, listed on the National Register, has garages attached to each room - standard back when every traveler wanted to keep an eye on their car. If you can book a room (reserve 2 months ahead in summer), it's the ultimate Route 66 experience.

activities.text: Walk through Tucumcari's neon lights - Over two dozen vintage neon signs illuminate the main street after dark. The Blue Swallow is the star, but also look for the Palomino Motel, Safari Motel, and Teepee Curios. Walk slowly - it's an open-air museum.

places.name: Tulsa

visits.text: Cars on the Route (Galena, KS) - Don't miss Route 66's brief passage through Kansas (only 13 miles) which runs through Galena, where an old International tow truck inspired the character Mater in Pixar's Cars. The shop is run by passionate volunteers.

Blue Whale (Catoosa, OK) - This 80-foot blue concrete whale, built in 1972 as an anniversary gift from a retiree to his wife, has become one of the most photographed icons of Route 66. You can climb on the whale's back. Free admission.

Tulsa - 11th Street (old Route 66) - Stay until evening - the vintage neon signs along old Route 66 through Tulsa light up one by one. The new Route 66 Experience Museum traces a century of Mother Road history with period cars and neon reconstructions.

activities.text: BBQ dinner in Tulsa - Oklahoma is barbecue country. Briskets smoked for 14 hours and ribs are served on butcher paper. Pair it with sweet iced tea - you're in the South, after all.
