places.name: Tusayan

visits.text: On the route to the Grand Canyon via US-89, stop at Cameron Trading Post, a trading post founded in 1916 on Navajo lands, beside the Little Colorado River. Its Navajo craft gallery—rugs, silver and turquoise jewelry, pottery—is one of the region's most comprehensive. The suspension footbridge behind the trading post offers a plunging view of the river's orange gorges.

Enter the park through the east entrance and first get a sense of the canyon from Desert View—the stone tower built in 1932 by architect Mary Colter on the South Rim stands at 7,480 feet. From there, the view extends some 20 miles of canyon, with the Colorado visible below. Then continue west on the Desert View Drive, stopping at Lipan Point: the river describes a curve rarely seen from such heights.

Mather Point is the South Rim's most frequented viewpoint—and for good reason: at 7,120 feet elevation, it plunges more than 4,500 feet above the Colorado, and on clear days you can see up to 60 miles west. It's named after Stephen Mather, the first director of the National Park Service. Arrive at dawn to watch the walls ignite—and to avoid the crowds that arrive by 9 a.m.

Walk along the Rim Trail to Yavapai Geology Museum (0.7 miles from Mather Point), built in 1928 from local limestone and ponderosa pine to blend into the landscape. Its panels trace 1.8 billion years of geological history—each yard of the canyon wall represents roughly one million years. The view through the museum's picture windows is among the South Rim's widest.

Continue west on the Rim Trail to Grand Canyon Village Historic District: the stone buildings constructed by Mary Colter in the 1930s (El Tovar Hotel, Lookout Studio, Bright Angel Lodge) are all designated historic landmarks. Step inside Lookout Studio, perched at the cliff's edge since 1914—the architecture plays with the rocks to the point where it's hard to tell where the building ends and the rock face begins.

activities.text: Position yourself at Mather Point or Yavapai Point for the golden hour and sunset—the canyon walls successively turn from yellow to blood red to orange depending on the light. If you arrive in late afternoon, it's not uncommon to see the colors shift within twenty minutes. Plan to be there 45 minutes before sunset.

Descend a few hundred yards on the Bright Angel Trail to feel the canyon from the inside—the first switchbacks reveal rock layers you couldn't see from above. Don't go below Indian Garden (9-mile round trip, too ambitious for a single day in high heat). The park recommends not descending below Two-Mile Resthouse in summer.

Take the free Hermit Road shuttle (Red Route) to Hermits Rest west of the village—7 miles of viewpoints with no private vehicles allowed, with stops like Pima Point and Monument Creek Vista. The shuttle runs year-round in the outbound direction, which allows you to alternate walking and riding the bus.

places.name: Twentynine Palms

visits.text: Joshua Tree National Park sits at the boundary between two deserts - the Mojave to the north, cooler, where Joshua trees grow (arborescent yuccas that can live 500 years), and the Colorado Desert to the south, hotter, populated with cholla and ocotillo. Hidden Valley is its geological heart: a closed cirque of quartz monzonite boulders rising from the ground over millions of years. Local legend says it was a cattle rustlers' hideout in the 19th century. Today it's mainly a climbing paradise - over 8,000 documented routes in the park, many starting from here. The 1.6-kilometer nature loop is accessible to all and passes beneath spectacular formations.

Skull Rock, accessible directly from the parking lot on Park Boulevard, is one of the park's most photographed rocks - depressions carved by water erosion give it the appearance of a skull. A 2.7-kilometer nature trail starts there and runs along several remarkable formations with interpretive panels on desert geology. It's a good introduction if you haven't done Hidden Valley.

activities.text: Late afternoon, descend toward the Cholla Cactus Garden on Pinto Basin Road - a transition zone between the two deserts where thousands of "teddy bear" chollas cover 4 hectares. Don't be fooled by their fluffy appearance: their hollow spines barb into skin at the slightest brush and curve once in contact with moisture, making them extremely difficult to remove. Stay on the trail and wear closed-toe shoes. Late afternoon light creates a golden halo around each cactus - it's the ideal time for photos.

Joshua Tree is one of the American West's finest parks for stargazing - a certified Dark Sky Park with no significant light pollution for dozens of kilometers in any direction. Park in one of the park's isolated parking areas after nightfall and look skyward. Tour operators offer guided astronomy outings from Twentynine Palms.

visits.text: Escape to Tybee Island, Savannah's laid-back beach retreat where wide sandy shores, rolling dunes, and tranquil marshlands create a carefree coastal atmosphere. The historic lighthouse, Georgia's oldest and tallest, has guided mariners since 1736 and offers panoramic views from its observation deck after climbing 178 steps. The island maintains nostalgic beach-town charm with colorful cottages and relaxed local culture.

Explore the imposing Fort Pulaski National Monument, a masterpiece of 19th-century military engineering whose massive brick walls were breached by rifled cannon during the Civil War, forever changing the course of warfare. The moated fortification and surrounding marshlands offer walking trails rich with birdlife and historical significance. Interpretive programs bring the fort's dramatic history to life.

activities.text: Swim and sunbathe at lively South Beach, spot dolphins offshore, and watch pelicans diving for fish as the sun sets over the Atlantic. The Tybee Pier and Pavilion area buzzes with activity while quieter stretches of shore provide peaceful escapes. Over 200 bird species make this island a rewarding destination for wildlife observation throughout the year.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: Oak Alley Plantation - The alley of 28 three-hundred-year-old live oaks leading to the mansion is the most iconic image of Louisiana. Planted between 1725 and 1750, these trees form a 250-meter tunnel of green. The house, built in 1837 by Jacques Télesphore Roman (brother of the governor), is a 21-meter square surrounded by 28 columns—as many as there are oak trees. Allow at least 2 hours. Don't miss the slavery exhibit in the reconstructed slave quarters: it's there you learn that an enslaved man named Antoine grafted the first commercial pecan trees in 1846.

Whitney Plantation - Twenty minutes from Oak Alley, this is the only Louisiana plantation entirely dedicated to the history of slavery. No romantic narrative here—the guided tour (mandatory) focuses on the lives of enslaved people, with testimonies carved in stone. Count on 1.5 hours.

activities.text: Drive the River Road - The road hugging the Mississippi between New Orleans and Baton Rouge passes a dozen plantations. The stretch between Vacherie and Donaldsonville is the most spectacular—historic mansions, sugar cane fields, and centuries-old oaks.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Louisiana Road Trip: Jazz and Bayous

visits.text: Jour 1: Worthington Glacier State Recreation Site - Located along Thompson Pass at 2,800 feet elevation, this glacier flows down from the Chugach Mountains to within easy viewing distance of the Richardson Highway. A paved trail leads to observation platforms, while more adventurous visitors can hike the two-mile Worthington Glacier Trail for closer access to the ice.

Jour 1: Bridal Veil Falls - This spectacular 900-foot cascade plunges down sheer cliffs along Keystone Canyon, visible from roadside pullouts and accessible via a mile-long trail to an overlook. The waterfall is most impressive during spring snowmelt and after summer rains.

Jour 1: Keystone Canyon - The Richardson Highway threads through this dramatic gorge carved by the Lowe River, with towering rock walls and multiple waterfalls creating one of Alaska's most scenic drives. Historic remnants of the failed Trans-Alaska Railway attempt are visible along the canyon walls.

Jour 2: Valdez Harbor and Waterfront - This ice-free port at the head of a fjord serves as the terminus of the Trans-Alaska Pipeline and a major fishing harbor. The downtown area rebuilt after the 1964 earthquake offers views of surrounding peaks rising directly from the water.

Jour 2: Prince William Sound - This island-studded waterway encompasses 15,000 square miles of protected waters, tidewater glaciers, and abundant marine wildlife. The sound's deep fjords and sheltered bays create ideal conditions for viewing sea otters, seals, sea lions, and whales.

activities.text: Jour 1: Thompson Pass Scenic Drive - The approach to Valdez crosses this 2,678-foot pass through the Chugach Mountains, an area receiving some of North America's highest snowfall totals. In summer, alpine meadows burst with wildflowers while glaciers cling to surrounding peaks.

Jour 2: Glacier Cruise - Day cruises venture into Columbia Bay to witness Columbia Glacier, one of Alaska's largest tidewater glaciers. These journeys navigate through floating icebergs while naturalists explain glacial geology and point out wildlife including puffins, kittiwakes, and marine mammals.

Jour 2: Sea Kayaking - Guided paddling excursions explore the fjords and bays surrounding Valdez, offering intimate access to marine ecosystems inaccessible by larger vessels. Multi-day expeditions reach remote glaciers and camping beaches.

Jour 2: Wildlife Viewing - Prince William Sound's nutrient-rich waters support abundant marine life, with sea otters, harbor seals, Steller sea lions, humpback whales, and orcas regularly observed. Seabird colonies host thousands of puffins, cormorants, and eagles.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

visits.text: Head to Vicksburg via the Natchez Trace Parkway - this old Native American trading path, which became a federal road in 1801, is now a scenic route with no billboards or trucks. The first 100 kilometers from Natchez traverse cypress forests and swamps. Stop at Emerald Mound, one of the largest ceremonial mounds in North America (dating from 1250), just north of Natchez.

Vicksburg National Military Park commemorates the 47-day siege (May-July 1863) that gave the Union control of the Mississippi. The 16-mile scenic drive winds past more than 1,300 monuments and markers. Stop at the Illinois Memorial, inspired by Rome's Pantheon, and at the Kentucky Monument where statues of Lincoln and Jefferson Davis face each other - both were born in the same state. Allow 2 to 3 hours for the driving loop.

In the park, don't miss the USS Cairo, a 175-foot Union ironclad gunboat sunk in the Yazoo River in 1862 by a Confederate mine - the first ship in history sunk by a mine. It remained on the bottom for 102 years before being raised in 1964. You can walk on the deck and see the breach from the explosion. The adjacent museum displays objects recovered from the ship, including a bottle of hot sauce that was still edible when opened.

activities.text: Late in the day, visit the Old Court House Museum in downtown Vicksburg. This neo-Greek building from 1858 houses Civil War artifacts and tells the story of river commerce. From the cupola, the view of the Yazoo Canal and boat silhouettes at twilight is beautiful.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep South Road Trip: New Orleans and the Natchez Trace

visits.text: Virginia City — Step into this authentically preserved 1860s mining boomtown perched on Mount Davidson's slopes. The National Historic Landmark preserves boardwalks, Victorian buildings, and saloons from when Comstock Lode silver discovery created Nevada Territory.

The Way It Was Museum — Explore mining equipment, minerals, and artifacts explaining how the Comstock Lode produced 500 million dollars in silver and gold. The museum occupies the fourth floor of the 1876 Knights of Pythias building.

Chollar Mine — Descend into a real silver mine with original 1860s square-set timbering still supporting tunnels. Guided tours explain mining techniques that extracted precious metals from 300 meters underground.

St. Mary in the Mountains Catholic Church — Visit this 1876 Gothic Revival church built with mining wealth featuring Italian marble and stained glass. The cathedral replaced an earlier church destroyed in the Great Fire of 1875.

activities.text: C Street walking tour — Stroll the main street lined with saloons, shops, and museums occupying original 1860s buildings. Wooden boardwalks and Victorian storefronts recreate Wild West atmosphere.
Virginia & Truckee Railroad ride — Board vintage steam trains running the historic route built in 1869 to haul Comstock ore. The scenic journey passes mining ruins with mountain views.
Saloon experience — Sample local beers at authentic Old West saloons with swinging doors and antique bars. Some establishments maintain 1860s character serving spirits to modern visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: Nevada's Great Contrasts: Las Vegas Neon to Red Rock Desert Silence

visits.text: Wamego OZ Museum - Where a Kansas Farm Girl Became Hollywood Legend: This whimsical museum houses over 2,000 artifacts telling the story of how L. Frank Baum's children's novel about a tornado-swept Kansas girl became one of the most-watched films in cinema history.

OZ Museum Collection - Original 1939 Movie Artifacts: Browse signed photographs by the film's stars, original L. Frank Baum books, and production memorabilia from the beloved 1939 movie that made 'There's no place like home' an American catchphrase.

activities.text: Downtown Wamego - Small Town Charm on the Prairie: Explore the charming main street of this Kansas community that has embraced its connection to one of America's most beloved stories.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

places.name: Washington

visits.text: Smithsonian Institution Building (The Castle) - Your museum marathon begins at the founding building of the Smithsonian, but be warned: the Castle has been under renovation since February 2023 (its first major overhaul in 50 years). A temporary reopening is planned from May 22 to September 7, 2026 for the United States' 250th anniversary celebrations. If you're there during this window, you'll be able to visit the visitor center and the American Aspirations exhibition. This neo-Gothic red sandstone castle, designed by James Renwick Jr. (the same architect behind New York's St. Patrick's Cathedral), was completed in 1855. Don't miss James Smithson's crypt - this British scientist who never set foot in the United States bequeathed his entire fortune to establish what became the world's largest museum complex. His remains were brought back from Genoa in 1905. If the Castle is closed during your visit, admire the exterior facade and use the virtual visitor center on si.edu to plan your visits.

Enid A. Haupt Garden - Right behind the Castle, this 4-acre garden is a little gem many visitors miss. Since 1987, it has featured brick walkways, fountains, and meticulously maintained flower beds. The underground entrances to the African Art Museum and the Asian Art Museum are located here. It's the perfect spot to map out your program before tackling the museums.

National Museum of American History - This is where you'll see the Star-Spangled Banner, the original 9-by-10-meter flag that flew over Fort McHenry in 1814 and inspired the national anthem. This wool and cotton flag, sewn by Mary Pickersgill in Baltimore, is preserved in a special chamber with floor-to-ceiling glass walls. Note that photography is prohibited in this room. The museum houses 1.7 million objects - you won't be able to see everything in one day.

National Air and Space Museum - One of the world's most visited museums holds the planet's largest collection of aircraft and spacecraft: approximately 60,000 objects. Right from the entrance, the Boeing Milestones of Flight Hall puts you face-to-face with the showstoppers. Plan for at least 3 to 4 hours, and book your free timed-entry pass online - it's required.

National Museum of Natural History - The world's most visited natural history museum welcomes you with Henry, a 4-meter-tall, 11-ton mounted African elephant that has stood in the rotunda since 1959. The museum is larger than 18 football fields and houses the world's largest natural history collection. Free admission, no reservation required.

National Museum of African American History and Culture - Opened in 2016, this is the only national museum entirely devoted to African American history. The 10-story building (5 above ground, 5 below) is clad in a pierced bronze crown inspired by West African decorative metalwork. With more than 45,000 objects in its collection (3,500 on display), plan for a full day. Book your free timed-entry pass online - it's essential, as the museum often sells out.

National Museum of the American Indian - This building with golden limestone curves, designed to evoke natural rock formations, houses collections devoted to the indigenous peoples of the three Americas. The architecture itself deserves attention: prismatic windows cast light onto the interior walls, and a 36-meter-high central dome crowns the entrance hall. The outdoor space recreates a wetland landscape with plants used by indigenous nations.

National Museum of African Art - This underground museum, accessible from the Haupt Garden, is the only American national museum devoted exclusively to African art. You'll discover wood and bronze sculptures, textiles, ceremonial masks, and ceramics spanning millennia of artistic creation. The contemporary African collections are particularly strong - don't expect only ancient art.

National Museum of Asian Art (Freer Gallery and Arthur M. Sackler Gallery) - Connected by an underground passage, these two galleries form the Smithsonian's Asian art museum. The Freer Gallery holds the world's most important collection of Whistler's works, including the Peacock Room: a London reception room entirely decorated by James McNeill Whistler in 1877 - blue-green and gold walls, life-size painted peacocks, transported from London piece by piece. The Sackler Gallery displays Chinese ritual bronzes dating from the Shang Dynasty (around 1500 BC), Japanese ceramics, Mughal Indian miniatures, and calligraphy. Don't miss the Moongate Garden between the two buildings - a circular Chinese-inspired garden perfect for a break.

Smithsonian American Art Museum - Housed in the former Patent Office Building (an 1836 Greek Revival structure), this museum traces three centuries of American art. You'll see canvases by Georgia O'Keeffe, Edward Hopper, Winslow Homer, and Hudson River School artists. The folk art collection is one of the most important in the country. On the 3rd floor, the Luce Foundation Center displays more than 3,300 objects in glass cases - a visible storage facility open to the public.

National Portrait Gallery - In the same building (both museums share the Patent Office Building), the National Portrait Gallery displays portraits of significant figures in American history. Each president has their official portrait here. The interior courtyard covered by a Norman Foster-designed undulating glass roof is a perfect place to catch your breath between galleries.

Renwick Gallery - Steps from the White House, this Smithsonian American Art Museum gallery occupies an elegant 1859 Second Empire building, also designed by James Renwick Jr. It's devoted to contemporary American craft: blown glass, carved wood, textiles, ceramics, metalwork. Monumental installations often occupy entire rooms - the Grand Salon on the 2nd floor, with its salon-style floor-to-ceiling paintings, is worth seeing on its own.

National Postal Museum - Right across from Union Station, this often-overlooked museum is a gem. Housed in the former Washington main post office (a 1914 Beaux-Arts building), it traces the history of the American postal service from Pony Express riders to mail planes. You'll see stagecoaches, historic mailboxes, mail planes suspended from the ceiling, and one of the world's largest stamp collections. A playful museum, perfect if you're visiting with children.

Union Station - Even though it's not a museum, Washington's main train station deserves a stop. Inaugurated in 1907, it's a Beaux-Arts masterpiece with a 29-meter-high gilded coffered vault inspired by Rome's Baths of Diocletian.

Smithsonian's National Zoo - The National Zoo spans 66 hectares in the Woodley Park neighborhood, right in the heart of the city. Founded in 1889, it's one of the oldest zoos in the United States. It houses approximately 2,200 animals from 400 species, one-fifth of which are threatened. Like all Smithsonian sites, admission is free - but you must book a timed-entry pass online. Take the Metro (Woodley Park or Cleveland Park stations, red line).

Steven F. Udvar-Hazy Center (Chantilly, Virginia) - The only excursion outside Washington on this itinerary, and it's well worth the trip. This giant 70,000-square-meter hangar near Dulles Airport is the Air and Space Museum's annex - it houses aircraft too large for the Mall museum. You'll see the Space Shuttle Discovery (39 space missions over 27 years of service), the Lockheed SR-71 Blackbird (the fastest plane ever built, capable of flying at over Mach 3.3), the B-29 Enola Gay that dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima, and an Air France Concorde. Admission is free, parking costs $15. To get there by public transit, take the Metro's Silver Line to Innovation Center, then Fairfax Connector bus 983 which drops you at the entrance.

Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden - This 70-meter-diameter concrete cylinder, perched on four pillars 4 meters above ground, is Washington's first contemporary art museum. Designed by Gordon Bunshaft and opened in 1974, it houses more than 12,000 works. The collection was built from collector Joseph H. Hirshhorn's 1966 donation of 6,000 pieces. You'll see Picasso, Warhol, Rothko, de Kooning, Hopper. The visit flows naturally - you follow the circle on each floor.

Arts and Industries Building - Right next to the Castle, this Victorian-style building completed in 1881 is the oldest museum on the Mall. Designed by Adolf Cluss (who had already renovated the Castle after the 1865 fire), it hosted President Garfield's inaugural ball before it was even finished. After years of closure, it reopens intermittently for temporary exhibitions. The interior architecture is worth the trip: wrought-iron structure, vast halls of natural light, and decorative columns.

The National Mall memorials - For your last day, step out of the museums and take time to visit the monuments. The Lincoln Memorial (1922) with its 6-meter-high marble statue is at the west end of the Mall - this is where Martin Luther King delivered his "I Have a Dream" speech in 1963. The Washington Monument, a 169-meter obelisk completed in 1884, offers a panoramic view of the entire city (book free tickets online). The Vietnam Veterans Memorial (a black granite wall bearing the 58,318 names of soldiers killed) and the Korean War Veterans Memorial complete the circuit.

activities.text: Get your bearings on the National Mall - Use this first day to walk along the Mall and scout out the museums you'll visit in the coming days. From the Lincoln Memorial to the Capitol, you have about 3 km of walkway lined with free museums. Pass by the Washington Monument and the memorials to get an overview before diving into the collections.

Explore the pop culture collections - On the 3rd floor, you'll find the famous ruby slippers Dorothy wore in The Wizard of Oz, Abraham Lincoln's top hat, Muhammad Ali's boxing gloves, and Duke Ellington's sheet music. The Entertainment Nation gallery brings together 60 objects from American pop culture in 670 square meters. Don't miss Julia Child's kitchen either, reconstructed piece by piece.

Browse the presidential and First Ladies galleries - The American Presidency exhibition traces the history of presidents through personal and official objects. Right next door, the First Ladies gallery showcases the inaugural gowns worn at inaugural balls. You'll also see the desk on which Jefferson drafted the Declaration of Independence.

See the icons of aviation - The 1903 Wright Flyer stands at the center of its gallery: it's the very first powered aircraft to fly. During its 4th test at Kitty Hawk on December 17, 1903, piloted by Wilbur, it covered 852 feet (255.6 meters) in 59 seconds. Just above, Lindbergh's Spirit of St. Louis hangs suspended - the cockpit door is slightly ajar, at Lindbergh's own request. You'll also see the Blériot XI that crossed the English Channel in 1909.

Explore the conquest of space - You'll be able to touch a real piece of lunar rock brought back by the Apollo 17 mission. The suit Neil Armstrong wore during his first steps on the Moon is displayed here. Also look for John Glenn's Mercury Friendship 7 capsule (first American in orbit, 1962), the Apollo lunar module, and the F-1 engine from the Saturn V rocket. If you have time, the planetarium and IMAX theater are worth a visit.

Marvel at the Hope Diamond and the gem collection - In the Janet Annenberg Hooker Hall of Geology, Gems and Minerals, you'll see the Hope Diamond: a 45.52-carat deep blue diamond. This diamond was part of the French Crown Jewels under the name "Blue Diamond" since its purchase by Louis XIV in 1668. It was stolen during the French Revolution, recut, and changed hands several times before being donated to the Smithsonian in 1958. The hall also displays the Star of Asia sapphire, the Gachala emerald, and more than 7,500 individual gems. A sample from the asteroid Bennu is also on display.

Explore the Deep Time Hall of Fossils - The David H. Koch Hall of Fossils spans nearly 2,900 square meters and traces 4.6 billion years of Earth's history. You'll see 46 complete dinosaur specimens, including a T. rex and a Triceratops. The paleontology lab is visible from the gallery - you can watch scientists extract fossils in real time. Head up to the Sant Ocean Hall to see a life-size replica of a North Atlantic right whale.

Descend into the underground history galleries - The museum is visited from bottom to top, moving forward chronologically. On the underground levels, you'll traverse the history of slavery with a cabin from an Edisto Island plantation (South Carolina), Nat Turner's Bible (who led a slave rebellion in 1831), a segregated railroad car, and a guard tower from Angola Prison in Louisiana. You'll also see remnants of the São José, a slave ship discovered off the coast of South Africa.

Explore the culture and sports galleries - On the upper floors, the tone shifts. You'll find the shawl Queen Victoria gave to Harriet Tubman, Chuck Berry's red Cadillac, Gabby Douglas's leotard, Jesse Owens's spikes (1936 Olympics), and nine Olympic medals from Carl Lewis. The Musical Crossroads gallery traces African American influence from blues to hip-hop. The piano from August Wilson's play The Piano Lesson is also on display - a piano on which an enslaved man had carved his family's history.

Browse the exhibitions on indigenous nations - The Americans exhibition traces the presence of Native Americans in popular culture: sports team names, films, brands, place names. If you're going with children, the imagiNATIONS space offers interactive activities. The permanent collections feature more than 800,000 objects ranging from the Arctic to Tierra del Fuego.

Lunch at the Mitsitam Café - This is probably the best cafeteria of all the Smithsonian museums. The menu offers dishes inspired by indigenous culinary traditions, with five themed stations (North, South, Great Plains, Forests, Meso-America). A real meal, not just a sandwich break.

Compare artistic traditions from two continents - Take advantage of this day to observe parallels between African and Asian art: Benin bronzework versus Chinese ritual bronzes, Congolese Kuba textiles versus Japanese silks. Both museums are less crowded compared to the Mall's giants - you'll have peace and quiet.

Wander through the Penn Quarter neighborhood - Leaving the museum, you're in one of Washington's liveliest neighborhoods. Take the opportunity to explore the area's art galleries and Chinatown, whose grand entrance arch is just steps away. It's also a good neighborhood for dinner.

View the White House from Lafayette Square - Leaving the Renwick, cross the street: you're facing the White House. Lafayette Square offers the clearest view of the north facade. Take time to walk around via the Ellipse on the south side for another perspective. You can't go inside, but the proximity is striking.

Stroll along Pennsylvania Avenue - From the White House to the Capitol, Pennsylvania Avenue is Washington's ceremonial artery. This is where presidential motorcades pass on inauguration day. Along the way, you'll pass the Old Post Office, the FBI Building, and the National Archives (where the Declaration of Independence and Constitution are displayed - free admission).

Explore the Capitol Hill neighborhood - From Union Station, head toward the Capitol through residential streets lined with Victorian brick houses. This is locals' Washington, far from the touristy Mall. The Eastern Market (open Saturdays and Sundays) is a good spot for fresh produce, crafts, and street food.

See giant pandas Bao Li and Qing Bao - The two pandas, who arrived from China in October 2024, have been the zoo's stars since their public debut in January 2025. Bao Li (born August 4, 2021) is the grandson of Mei Xiang and Tian Tian, who lived here for 23 years. Qing Bao (born September 12, 2021) is more independent - you'll often find her perched in trees. The Smithsonian's panda program has lasted more than 50 years and helped move the species from "endangered" to "vulnerable" status.

Walk the Asia Trail and other exhibits - The Asia Trail brings together seven Asian species: sloth bears, red pandas, clouded leopards, otters, and more. Continue to the great apes, Asian elephants (in the Elephant Trails habitat opened in 2013), big cats, and the aviary. Plan for the morning - animals are more active before noon.

Climb the Donald D. Engen observation tower - From the top, you have a panoramic view of Dulles Airport runways and surroundings. It's also a chance to watch planes take off and land - a spectacle that fascinates children and adults alike.

Watch historic aircraft restoration - The Mary Baker Engen Restoration Hangar is visible from a glass-walled balcony. You can watch Smithsonian specialists restore historic aircraft - precision work that sometimes takes years per plane.

Visit the sculpture garden - The 5,300-square-meter Sculpture Garden, sunken 2 to 4 meters below street level, is a space unto itself. You'll find Rodin's Burghers of Calais - one of twelve original castings of this 1889 bronze sculpture depicting six notables offering their lives to save their city during the Hundred Years' War. Also look for Alexander Calder's mobiles and stabiles, Henry Moore's organic forms, and sculptures by Matisse, Giacometti, and Jeff Koons.

Discover Barbara Kruger's permanent installation - On the lower level, Belief+Doubt covers walls, floors, and staircases with black, white, and red texts that question power, consumption, and beliefs. It's one of Washington's most photographed installations.

Taste Washington's culinary specialties - Use this penultimate day to explore DC's food scene. The half-smoke is the iconic local sausage, half-pork half-beef, served grilled with mustard and onions. Mumbo sauce (a sweet-spicy condiment typical of DC) accompanies fried chicken wings. And if you haven't tried Ethiopian food yet, the area around U Street is home to the largest Ethiopian community outside Africa - the injera and spicy stews are remarkable.

Stroll through the Mall gardens - The Mary Livingston Ripley Garden (between the Hirshhorn and the Arts and Industries Building) and the Katherine Dulin Folger Rose Garden (behind the Castle) are two small gardens often overlooked. In late afternoon, the Mall empties out and the golden light on museum facades is superb.

Walk around the Tidal Basin - If you're visiting between late March and mid-April, the cherry blossoms around the Tidal Basin are an extraordinary sight - approximately 3,000 trees gifted by Japan in 1912. Even out of season, the loop around the basin (about 3 km) passes the Jefferson Memorial, the Martin Luther King Jr. Memorial, and the Franklin Delano Roosevelt Memorial. Allow 1.5 hours to see everything at a relaxed pace.

Final Mall pass at sunset - The Mall is even more beautiful in late afternoon. The low light on the Capitol at one end and the Lincoln Memorial at the other creates a panorama you won't forget. It's the best way to conclude 15 days at the heart of American history and culture.

visits.text: Explore picturesque Waynesville, sandwiched between the Great Smoky Mountains and the Blue Ridge Mountains. This charming little town in Haywood County captivates visitors with sublime mountain views and a welcoming atmosphere that embodies the spirit of Southern Appalachian hospitality.

Catch an entertaining live performance at Haywood Arts Regional Theater, where talented local and regional artists showcase their skills. This celebrated venue brings cultural enrichment to the mountain community and offers visitors an authentic taste of Appalachian arts and entertainment.

activities.text: Experience downtown Waynesville's bustling atmosphere featuring an eclectic mix of galleries, antique shops, and local boutiques. Visit Haywood's Historic Farmers Market to meet hospitable locals, sample fresh farm produce, and discover unique heritage crafts that make perfect mementos of your mountain visit.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

places.name: West Branch

visits.text: Herbert Hoover National Historic Site—The 31st U.S. President was born in this tiny two-room house in 1874. His father was a blacksmith. The original cottage, remarkably small, gives a tangible sense of the modest beginnings. Around it, a tallgrass prairie has been recreated to show the landscape Hoover saw as a child.

Herbert Hoover Presidential Library & Museum—The museum traces the life of a man who lived many lives: mining engineer in Australia and China, humanitarian relief coordinator during World War I, then president during the Great Depression. The Depression exhibit is especially well done.

Walk the site's loop trail—the blacksmith shop, Quaker meetinghouse, and the graves of Herbert and Lou Hoover, on a wooded hilltop overlooking the site, can be seen in 30 minutes. The grounds are peaceful and well-maintained.

activities.text: Stroll through the recreated tallgrass prairie—In summer, the grasses reach shoulder height and wildflowers attract butterflies and birds. A marked trail loops around in 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs

map_keywords: Herbert Hoover National Historic Site West Branch

visits.text: Glacier National Park — Enter this 4,100-square-kilometer wilderness preserving pristine ecosystems from prairie to alpine peaks. The Crown of the Continent features 700 lakes, 25 active glaciers, and abundant wildlife including grizzlies, wolves, and mountain goats.

Going-to-the-Sun Road — Drive this engineering marvel crossing the Continental Divide at 2,026-meter Logan Pass. The 80-kilometer scenic road completed in 1932 hugs cliff faces with stunning glacier and valley views.

Lake McDonald — Photograph the park's largest lake stretching 16 kilometers with mountains reflected in pristine waters. The glacially-carved lake reaches 141 meters deep surrounded by old-growth cedar and hemlock forests.

Trail of the Cedars — Walk this wheelchair-accessible boardwalk through ancient rainforest featuring 500-year-old western red cedars. The 1.1-kilometer loop crosses Avalanche Creek on a suspension bridge.

Logan Pass — Hike from the Continental Divide summit where snow persists into July. The alpine visitor center offers exhibits and access to Hidden Lake Trail through wildflower meadows frequented by mountain goats.
Hidden Lake Overlook — Trek 4.8 kilometers roundtrip to dramatic views of turquoise Hidden Lake surrounded by peaks. The trail crosses alpine tundra revealing bighorn sheep and hoary marmots.
Jackson Glacier Overlook — View one of Glacier's most accessible glaciers from this roadside pullout. The ice field has receded significantly but remains impressive against the Continental Divide.
St. Mary Falls — Hike 2.6 kilometers through forest to this 10-meter waterfall cascading over bedrock. The trail follows Rose Creek through wildflower meadows with mountain views.

Lewis and Clark Caverns State Park — Tour Montana's largest known limestone cavern featuring stalactites, stalagmites, and helictites. The 90-minute guided walk descends into chambers carved by water over millions of years.
Gates of the Mountains — Boat through this section of the Missouri River where limestone cliffs rise 370 meters. Lewis and Clark named the formation in 1805 during their expedition upriver.
Helena historic downtown — Explore Montana's capital featuring Victorian architecture from gold rush prosperity. Last Chance Gulch preserves 1880s buildings housing shops and restaurants.

activities.text: Wildlife watching — Scan meadows and roadside for grizzly bears, black bears, bighorn sheep, and mountain goats. Early morning and evening offer best viewing opportunities along Going-to-the-Sun Road.
Apgar Village exploration — Browse visitor center exhibits, rent boats, and dine at this lakeside village. The park's west entrance hub offers services and lake access.
Scenic boat tour — Cruise historic wooden boats across pristine lakes hearing park history and geology from naturalist guides. Tours operate on Lake McDonald and St. Mary Lake.
Evening ranger program — Attend talks about grizzly bears, glaciers, or night skies at park amphitheaters. Rangers share ecological knowledge and conservation challenges.
Huckleberry treats — Sample Montana's signature berry in ice cream, pie, or jam at park lodges. Wild huckleberries ripen in late summer throughout Glacier's forests.
Scenic drive return — Navigate mountain passes and valleys back to Glacier through heart of the Rockies. The journey showcases Montana's vast wilderness and big sky.
Farewell Montana dinner — Celebrate the journey with bison ribeye or grilled trout at a mountain lodge. Montana cuisine emphasizes wild game and locally raised meats.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers
Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers
Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers

visits.text: West Palm Beach - Florida's Emerging Cultural Capital: This rapidly developing city has cultivated world-class dining, entertainment venues, and high-end shopping while maintaining easy access to pristine Atlantic beaches.

Palm Beach - Billionaire's Row: Across the Intracoastal Waterway, Palm Beach proper showcases some of America's most valuable real estate, with beachfront mansions owned by the nation's wealthiest families.

John D. MacArthur Beach State Park - Preserved Paradise: This barrier island park protects one of the last remaining undeveloped stretches of the Palm Beach coast, with hiking trails, kayaking, and swimming on a pristine Atlantic beach.

Norton Museum of Art - World-Class Collection in the Tropics: This recently expanded museum houses significant European, American, and Chinese art collections in stunning galleries designed for natural light.

activities.text: Jupiter - Palm Beach's Best-Kept Secret: This small northern town offers outstanding beaches, historic lighthouse tours, and a laid-back atmosphere that contrasts with Palm Beach's formality.
Delray Beach and Boca Raton - Atlantic Avenue Strolling: These neighboring beach communities offer vibrant downtown districts filled with restaurants, boutiques, and galleries steps from the ocean.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Experience White Sands National Park, where the world's largest gypsum dune field creates a surreal landscape of brilliant white waves stretching across 275 square miles of the Tularosa Basin. The pristine white dunes, constantly shifting with the wind, provide an otherworldly environment unlike anywhere else on Earth. This rare ecosystem supports uniquely adapted plants and animals found nowhere else.

Walk the Interdune Boardwalk and Dune Life Nature Trail to discover how life persists in this seemingly barren landscape of pure white gypsum crystals. Interpretive signs explain the geological processes that created this remarkable feature and the adaptations that allow plants and animals to survive in such an extreme environment.

activities.text: Sled down the pristine white dunes on specially designed sand discs available at the visitor center, creating unforgettable memories in one of the most photogenic landscapes in America. Sunset transforms the white sands into a canvas of pink, orange, and purple hues, offering photographers extraordinary opportunities for capturing the desert's ethereal beauty.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

visits.text: Start with downtown Whitefish—Architectural Digest named it one of America's 25 most beautiful small towns, and you'll quickly see why. Central Avenue packs art galleries, boutiques, and restaurants into an authentic mountain-town setting. Don't miss the Whitefish Depot, the historic rail station where Amtrak's Empire Builder (Chicago-Seattle line) still stops. The small museum inside chronicles the town's history as a railroad settlement founded in the early twentieth century.

Head down to City Beach, the public beach at the end of Whitefish Lake. The lake stretches 11 km long by 4 km wide, with a maximum depth of 68 meters. The water runs clear and the beach is lifeguarded in summer—you'll see families, paddleboarders, and kayakers. If you prefer a wilder setting, push on to Whitefish Lake State Park on the west shore.

Last big hiking day: rise early to tackle the Iceberg Lake trail, one of the finest hikes in all of Glacier National Park. The trailhead sits behind Swiftcurrent Motor Inn in the Many Glacier valley. Figure 16 km round-trip with 390 m elevation gain—it's rated moderate and doable in 5 to 6 hours. The trail crosses fir forests, wildflower meadows, and follows the Ptarmigan Wall, a 450-meter-high ridge carved by glaciers. At the end, Iceberg Lake awaits at 1,858 m elevation, wedged between 900-meter cliffs—ice blocks float on its surface even in midsummer because sunlight almost never reaches the water. If you're brave, dip your feet—the water is frigid but invigorating.

The Many Glacier area is the park's best spot for observing grizzly bears. The Iceberg Lake trail corridor is particularly active in summer when huckleberry bushes are loaded with berries. Make noise while walking, keep your bear spray accessible (not in your backpack), and hike in a group if possible. If the trail is closed for bear activity (this happens regularly in July-August), fall back on the Cracker Lake Trail (19 km R/T) whose intensely turquoise lake is worth the effort.

Last morning in Whitefish before departure. If your flight is late in the day, ride the gondola to the summit of Whitefish Mountain Resort (15 minutes' drive from downtown). In summer the resort offers scenic lift rides, a zip-line course (Montana's longest), alpine slides, and a network of mountain biking trails. From the Summit House at the top, the view sweeps across the entire Flathead Valley with the mountains of Glacier Park in the distance.

If you'd rather stay in town, the Whitefish Trail offers a network of hiking and biking trails circling the town. The Living Wetland Interpretive Nature Trail loop is easy and short—perfect for a last peaceful walk through the wetlands bordering the Whitefish River.

activities.text: Spend the evening touring downtown's craft breweries. Whitefish has several microbreweries that close at 8pm (Montana law for craft breweries). Sample the huckleberry specialties—this wild local berry shows up in beers, ice creams, and even vodkas.

Pack your gear for the coming days: buy bear spray at one of the downtown outdoor shops (figure around forty dollars), verify your camping reservations if you're camping, and note that gas isn't available inside Glacier National Park.

After the hike, treat yourself to ice cream at the Swiftcurrent Motor Inn—you'll have earned it. Then take the road back to Whitefish via US-89 south and US-2 west (about 2.5 hours driving). Enjoy the evening dining at one of Whitefish's downtown restaurants. The town is known for its culinary scene—several local chefs have earned James Beard Foundation Award nominations.

Make a final tour of Central Avenue's shops to pick up souvenirs—Montana t-shirts and huckleberry products are the classics. Glacier Park International Airport (FCA) is only 15 minutes' drive from Whitefish, giving you plenty of margin to enjoy the morning.

visits.text: Wichita - Kansas's Cultural and Culinary Capital: Five culturally rich museums line the Arkansas River, connected by a riverfront walkway that makes exploring this diverse city a pleasure.
Old Cowtown Museum - Wichita's Frontier Days Brought to Life: Costumed actors recapture the city's Wild West heritage in this living history museum, complete with period buildings and demonstrations.
Exploration Place - 10,000+ Works and 1950s Kansas in Miniature: Interactive exhibits include a miniature version of 1950s Kansas with more than 50 animations, bringing mid-century America to life.
Wichita Art Museum - American Art on the Arkansas: Over 10,000 works of American art fill this riverside institution, one of the largest art museums in the Great Plains.

Keeper of the Plains - 44 Feet of Iconic Sculpture: Local artist Blackbear Bosin's dramatic steel sculpture rises at the confluence of the Arkansas and Little Arkansas rivers, illuminated nightly by the Ring of Fire.

Mid-America All-Indian Museum - Native Heritage on Display: More works by Blackbear Bosin and extensive collections celebrating Native American art and culture await visitors to this riverside institution.

Botanica Wichita - 30+ Themed Gardens Open Year-Round: Sculptures, water features, 4,000 plant species, and a restored 1949 carousel accent this botanical garden that culminates annually with 2 million holiday lights.

activities.text: Arkansas River Walk - Connect Wichita's Cultural Treasures: Stroll, bike, or jog along the riverfront pathway linking museums, sculptures, and green spaces.

Bartlett Arboretum in Belle Plaine - A Prairie Rarity Flourishes: Thirty minutes south of Wichita, this century-old wooded haven hosts events beginning each April with Art at the Arb amid 40,000 tulips.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: Stroll along the historic Riverwalk in downtown Wilmington, a picturesque waterfront promenade stretching along the Cape Fear River. This charming walkway winds through the city's beautifully preserved historic district, offering stunning river views, unique shops, and glimpses of antebellum architecture. The Riverwalk perfectly captures the essence of Southern coastal charm.

activities.text: Explore Wilmington's historic downtown district, where centuries-old buildings house boutiques, galleries, and eateries along tree-lined streets. The city's rich maritime heritage and well-preserved architecture make it one of North Carolina's most photogenic destinations. Take time to admire the blend of antebellum mansions and waterfront charm that defines this coastal gem.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Wilson Lake - The Clearest Lake in Kansas: Red sandstone cliffs and pillars interrupt the shoreline of this remarkably clear reservoir, creating scenery more reminiscent of the Southwest than the Great Plains.

Wilson State Park - Where Smoky Hills Meet Crystal Waters: Lake lovers fish, paddle, boat, and swim while mountain bikers conquer the 25-mile Switchgrass Trail that snakes through canyons carved into the surrounding Smoky Hills.

activities.text: Switchgrass Trail - 25 Miles of Mountain Biking Adventure: This challenging trail system winds through Smoky Hills canyons, offering technical terrain and scenic views rarely associated with Kansas.

Cheney Lake Sailing - Kansas's Premier Wind Sports Destination: Steady prairie breezes have made this lake one of the nation's top sailing and windsurfing destinations, drawing a dedicated seafaring following.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: New England Air Museum - The Largest Aviation Collection in the Northeast: Explore an extraordinary assemblage of aircraft, helicopters, engines, and memorabilia spanning the entire history of flight. Highlights include the oldest surviving aircraft in America (a hot air balloon basket) and the oldest Connecticut-constructed airplane.

Historic Aircraft Collection - From Pioneers to Jets: Walk among vintage fighters, bombers, and experimental aircraft in massive hangars, with knowledgeable volunteer docents-many former pilots and aviation professionals-ready to share stories behind each machine.

activities.text: Redbird Flight Simulator - Take the Controls: Experience the thrill of piloting through this realistic simulator, while younger visitors can build paper airplanes and participate in family-friendly aviation scavenger hunts throughout the facility.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven

places.name: Winslow

visits.text: El Rancho Hotel (Gallup, NM) - This 1937 motel, nicknamed the 'Playground of the Stars,' hosted Hollywood stars during western film shoots in the 1940s-50s. Each room is named after a star - John Wayne, Katharine Hepburn, Ronald Reagan. Even if you don't stay overnight, the lobby and its hunting trophies are worth a look.

Petrified Forest National Park - Don't miss this park protecting the world's largest concentration of petrified wood: 225-million-year-old tree trunks transformed into colorful quartz crystals. The 28-mile scenic drive also crosses the Painted Desert - hills striped with red, purple, and white. Don't take anything: it's illegal and the park estimates it loses 12 tons of petrified wood annually to visitors.

Standing on the Corner Park (Winslow, AZ) - 'Take it easy, take it easy / Standing on the corner in Winslow, Arizona...' - the 1972 Eagles song saved this town from decline. A bronze statue and mural mark the street corner. Five-minute photo stop, but the song will stick in your head all day.

activities.text: Stay at La Posada (Winslow) - If you can book a room at this 1929 former Harvey House hotel, designed by architect Mary Colter, it's the most beautiful lodging on the entire Route 66. The Turquoise Room restaurant serves refined Southwest cuisine. Even without staying overnight, visit the gardens and lobby.

visits.text: Upper Dells Sandstone Gorges - The Wisconsin River carved these canyons through 500-million-year-old Cambrian sandstone. The walls rise 100-130 feet and take spectacular shapes: columns, arches, mushroom rocks. The only way to see them is by boat - the shores are private and protected.

Witches Gulch - Accessible only from the cruise, this narrow gorge is carpeted with moss and ferns. A wooden walkway winds between sandstone walls so close together there's almost never direct sunlight. Photographer H.H. Bennett discovered and named it in the 1860s - his photos launched tourism at Wisconsin Dells.

Stand Rock - An isolated sandstone pillar facing a cliff. Since 1886, a trained dog has jumped the gap between cliff and rock to recreate Bennett's legendary photograph. It's Wisconsin's most iconic image.

activities.text: Upper Dells Cruise - The 2-hour tour goes upriver between sandstone cliffs, with two land stops: Witches Gulch and Stand Rock. The guide tells Native American legends, raftsmen (log floaters) history, and canyon geology. Departs from downtown.
Lower Dells Cruise (optional) - The 1-hour tour goes downriver passing Hawk's Bill, the Rocky Islands, and other formations. Less spectacular than the Upper Dells but complementary.

visits.text: Guided tour of Wonderland Cave exploring limestone chambers, stalactites, and stalagmites formed over millions of years

activities.text: Browse the gift shop at Wonderland Cave for minerals, fossils, and local souvenirs

days_plan.itin_title: Black Hills & Badlands South Dakota Road Trip

visits.text: Columbia River Gorge — Cross this dramatic 1,200-meter-deep canyon where the Columbia River cuts through the Cascade Range. The 130-kilometer gorge creates the only sea-level route through the mountains, featuring over 77 waterfalls.

Multnomah Falls — Admire Oregon's tallest waterfall plunging 189 meters in two major tiers connected by the historic Benson Bridge built in 1914. The iconic cascade attracts over 2 million visitors annually to its easily accessible viewing platform.

Maryhill Museum of Art — Explore this eclectic collection housed in a 1914 mansion overlooking the Columbia River. Holdings include European paintings, Auguste Rodin sculptures, Native American artifacts, and Queen Marie of Romania's furnishings.

Yakima Valley wine country — Drive through Washington's premier wine region producing 40% of the state's grapes. The rain shadow of the Cascades creates ideal conditions for Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Chardonnay.

activities.text: Wine tasting tour — Visit family-owned wineries and tasting rooms sampling award-winning Washington wines. Many vineyards offer tours explaining viticulture practices and the valley's unique terroir.

Historic Highway 14 drive — Navigate this scenic route along the Columbia's north bank past basalt cliffs, orchards, and Native American fishing sites. The highway offers dramatic gorge views and access to small riverside communities.

Farm-to-table dinner — Savor cuisine featuring Yakima Valley produce including hops, apples, cherries, and wine. The agricultural region supplies ingredients to restaurants throughout the Pacific Northwest.

days_plan.itin_title: Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes

visits.text: Explore York's traditional Southern town center, where the historic York County Courthouse and prominent commercial buildings line streets that retain their 19th-century character. As part of South Carolina's Olde English District, the town connects to important Revolutionary War history. The McCelvey Center, housed in a handsome brick building, serves as a Southern Revolutionary War Institute with exhibits and programs.

activities.text: Enjoy the outdoors at Anne Springs Close Greenway, an expansive natural area well-suited for quiet afternoon hikes, horseback riding, and nature observation. The greenway's trails wind through diverse habitats providing peaceful escapes from busier destinations. York's combination of history and natural beauty offers a fitting conclusion to a South Carolina journey.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Yosemite National Park - America's Cathedral of Stone and Water: Sheer granite walls rise 3,000 feet from the valley floor as waterfalls plunge from heights that dwarf skyscrapers. This UNESCO World Heritage Site inspired the conservation movement and continues to humble millions of annual visitors.

El Capitan - The World's Most Famous Big Wall: This 3,000-foot vertical granite monolith draws elite climbers from around the globe attempting multi-day ascents of routes like The Nose. Even from the valley floor, watching climbers as tiny specks on the face is mesmerizing.

Half Dome - Yosemite's Iconic Sentinel: This distinctive granite dome, cleaved as if by a giant's axe, rises 4,737 feet above the valley floor. The cables route to the summit remains one of America's most sought-after hiking achievements.

Yosemite Falls - North America's Tallest Waterfall: Water plunges 2,425 feet in three tiers, creating a spectacle visible from throughout Yosemite Valley. Peak flow occurs in May and June when snowmelt transforms the falls into a thundering curtain of white.

activities.text: Tunnel View - The Most Photographed Vista in America: This overlook presents El Capitan, Half Dome, and Bridalveil Fall in a single sweeping panorama that has graced countless postcards, calendars, and desktop backgrounds.

Glacier Point - Stand 3,214 Feet Above the Valley: This dramatic overlook provides bird's-eye views of Yosemite's greatest hits, with Half Dome at eye level and the High Sierra stretching to the horizon.
Mariposa Grove - Walk Among Giant Sequoias: Over 500 mature giant sequoias populate this ancient forest, including the 1,800-year-old Grizzly Giant with its 96-foot circumference.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Enter Yosemite's high country via Tioga Road (seasonal) — alpine meadows, polished granite domes, and a world above the crowds.
Yosemite Valley loop trails or waterfall hikes — towering cliffs, thundering cascades, and the scent of pine and granite.
